> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST521 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Project Scheduling and Feasibility Study: Project Overview and Feasibility Studies, Identification, Market and Demand Analysis, Project Cost Estimate, Financial Appraisal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Project — The Foundational Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Project** is a *temporary* endeavour undertaken to create a *unique product, service, or result*. It has a definite beginning and end, defined scope, and allocated resources. In software engineering, a project is the coordinated set of activities that converts a stakeholder need into a working software system.

**Operational Attributes of a Project:**
- **Defined Start and End** — bounded in time.
- **Unique Deliverables** — no two projects are identical.
- **Progressive Elaboration** — details unfold as the project advances.
- **Resource Constrained** — limited budget, people, and time.
- **Stakeholder Driven** — driven by a sponsor/client.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for Board Exams:**
> A *Project* is **NOT** an *Operation*. Operations are ongoing and repetitive (e.g., payroll processing every month), whereas a project terminates upon delivering its objective (e.g., implementing a new payroll system).

### Intuitive Analogy — The "House Construction" Model
> Imagine you decide to build your dream house. You hire an architect, fix a budget of ₹50 Lakhs, set a deadline of 12 months, and want 3 bedrooms with a garden. The entire activity of *conception-to-handover* is a **Project**. Once you move in, the *routine of living* is an **Operation**. The unique nature, the finite timeline, the defined scope — that is what makes it a project.

---

## 1.2 Project Overview

A **Project Overview** is the executive-level summary that captures the *what, why, who, when, and how-much* of the proposed project before deep planning begins. It is typically the first deliverable of the **Project Initiation Phase**.

| Element | Description |
|---|---|
| Project Title | Unique name of the initiative |
| Sponsor | Authority sanctioning the project |
| Objective | Business problem being solved |
| Scope Boundaries | In-scope and out-of-scope items |
| Estimated Cost | Order-of-magnitude budget |
| Estimated Duration | Tentative timeline |
| Success Criteria | KPIs defining success |

---

## 1.3 Feasibility Study — The "Go / No-Go" Gateway

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Feasibility Study** (also called *Feasibility Analysis* or *Feasibility Report*) is a preliminary investigation conducted to determine whether the proposed project is *technically possible, operationally workable, financially viable, legally acceptable, and schedule-feasible* before significant resources are committed.

**Purpose:** It answers the single most important question — *"Should we proceed?"*

### The Five Pillars of Feasibility (MUST memorize for KTU exams)

1. **Technical Feasibility** — *Can we build it?*
   - Hardware, software, network, and skill availability.
2. **Operational Feasibility** — *Will it be used?*
   - User acceptance, organizational culture, training needs.
3. **Economic / Financial Feasibility** — *Is it worth the cost?*
   - Cost-benefit analysis, ROI, NPV, payback period.
4. **Legal Feasibility** — *Is it permissible?*
   - Compliance with laws, regulations, IP, contracts.
5. **Schedule Feasibility** — *Can it be done in time?*
   - Project deadline vs. available time window.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "GPS Before a Road Trip" Model
> Before driving 500 km to a hill station, you check fuel (technical feasibility), your driving licence (legal feasibility), budget for toll and food (financial feasibility), whether the roads are open in monsoon (schedule feasibility), and whether your family is willing to go (operational feasibility). A *Feasibility Study* does exactly this for a software project.

---

## 1.4 Project Identification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> **Project Identification** is the systematic process of recognizing, articulating, and shortlisting potential projects that align with the organization's strategic objectives. It is the *ideation-to-candidate* stage where business problems are converted into project opportunities.

**Common Sources of Project Identification:**
- Corporate strategic plans
- Market research findings
- Customer complaints / feedback
- Government regulations
- Technological advancements
- Competitive pressure

---

## 1.5 Market and Demand Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> **Market and Demand Analysis** is the study of the existing market structure, customer segments, competitor offerings, and projected demand for the proposed product/service. It determines the *revenue potential* of the project.

**Core Components:**
- **Market Size** (TAM, SAM, SOM)
- **Demand Forecasting** (qualitative + quantitative techniques)
- **Competitor Benchmarking**
- **Pricing Strategy**
- **SWOT Analysis**

### TAM / SAM / SOM (Must memorize)
- **TAM** — *Total Addressable Market* — the entire universe of revenue opportunity.
- **SAM** — *Serviceable Available Market* — the segment you can realistically target.
- **SOM** — *Serviceable Obtainable Market* — the share you can actually capture.

---

## 1.6 Project Cost Estimate

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> A **Project Cost Estimate** is a quantitative assessment of the monetary resources required to complete the project, including direct costs, indirect costs, and contingency reserves. It is the foundation of the financial feasibility pillar.

**Categories of Cost:**
- **Direct Costs** — labour, hardware, software licences.
- **Indirect Costs** — administration, utilities, overheads.
- **Sunk Costs** — already spent, irrelevant to future decisions.
- **Opportunity Costs** — benefits foregone by choosing one option.
- **Contingency Reserves** — buffer for known-unknowns.

---

## 1.7 Financial Appraisal

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> **Financial Appraisal** is the systematic evaluation of a project's financial viability using discounted cash flow techniques, ratios, and qualitative judgement to determine whether the project maximizes shareholder value.

**Key Tools Used:**
- **Net Present Value (NPV)**
- **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**
- **Payback Period (PBP)**
- **Return on Investment (ROI)**
- **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)**

---

## 1.8 Inter-relationship of Concepts (The Big Picture)

The following logical chain is **frequently asked in KTU 2-mark and 3-mark questions**:

$$\text{Strategic Plan} \rightarrow \text{Project Identification} \rightarrow \text{Market \& Demand Analysis} \rightarrow \text{Project Cost Estimate} \rightarrow \text{Financial Appraisal} \rightarrow \text{Feasibility Study} \rightarrow \text{Project Overview / Approval}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:**
> A *Feasibility Study* is the *output document* that integrates the *Project Identification*, *Market Analysis*, *Cost Estimate*, and *Financial Appraisal* into one cohesive go/no-go decision for the sponsor.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Anatomy of a Feasibility Study

A feasibility study is not a single document — it is a *multi-perspective investigation*. Each pillar has its own evidence base:

### 2.1.1 Technical Feasibility — Detailed View
**Questions Answered:**
- Does current infrastructure support the proposed system?
- Are required technologies (cloud, AI, blockchain, etc.) mature enough?
- Does the team possess the skills, or must we train/hire?
- Are third-party APIs and tools available and reliable?

**Outcome Indicators:**
- Technology readiness level (TRL)
- Proof-of-concept (PoC) results
- Skill-gap analysis percentage

### 2.1.2 Operational Feasibility — Detailed View
**Questions Answered:**
- Will end-users actually use the system?
- Does it align with organizational culture?
- What is the change-management burden?
- Are training and support resources adequate?

**Outcome Indicators:**
- User acceptance testing (UAT) readiness
- Stakeholder alignment score
- Resistance index

### 2.1.3 Economic / Financial Feasibility — Detailed View
**Questions Answered:**
- Is the projected ROI above the organization's hurdle rate?
- What is the payback period?
- What is the NPV at the given cost of capital?
- What are the recurring costs of ownership?

**Outcome Indicators:**
- NPV > 0
- IRR > hurdle rate
- PBP < acceptable threshold

### 2.1.4 Legal Feasibility — Detailed View
- Compliance with IT Act 2000 / DPDP Act 2023 (India-specific)
- Intellectual property rights
- Data privacy and protection regulations
- Licensing agreements (proprietary + open source)
- Contractual obligations with vendors

### 2.1.5 Schedule Feasibility — Detailed View
- Hard deadlines (regulatory, market-window)
- Resource availability windows
- Dependency analysis with concurrent projects
- Critical path length estimation

---

## 2.2 Project Cost Estimation — Detailed Techniques

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Techniques — Memorize the names and formulas.**

| # | Technique | When Used | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Analogous (Top-Down)** | Early phase, limited data | -50% to +50% |
| 2 | **Parametric** | Historical data available | -30% to +30% |
| 3 | **Bottom-Up (WBS-based)** | Detailed scope known | -10% to +10% |
| 4 | **Three-Point (PERT)** | High uncertainty | -15% to +15% |
| 5 | **COCOMO / COCOMO II** | Software-specific | -15% to +15% |
| 6 | **Expert Judgement / Delphi** | Innovative projects | Variable |

### 2.2.1 The Three-Point Estimation Formula (PERT)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula:**
> $$\text{Expected Estimate } (E) = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}$$
> $$\text{Standard Deviation } (\sigma) = \frac{P - O}{6}$$
> $$\text{Variance } (\sigma^2) = \left(\frac{P - O}{6}\right)^2$$

Where:
- **O** = Optimistic estimate
- **M** = Most likely estimate
- **P** = Pessimistic estimate

### 2.2.2 Basic COCOMO Formula (for software cost estimation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula:**
> $$\text{Effort } (E) = a \times (\text{KLOC})^b \text{ person-months}$$
> $$\text{Development Time } (D) = c \times (E)^d \text{ months}$$
> $$\text{Average Staff Size } (N) = \frac{E}{D}$$

| Project Type | a | b | c | d |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Organic | 2.4 | 1.05 | 2.5 | 0.38 |
| Semi-detached | 3.0 | 1.12 | 2.5 | 0.35 |
| Embedded | 3.6 | 1.20 | 2.5 | 0.32 |

---

## 2.3 Financial Appraisal — Master Formula Sheet

> [!CAUTION]
> **This is the single most testable area in Module 1 for KTU numerical problems.**

### 2.3.1 Net Present Value (NPV)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula:**
> $$\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1 + r)^t}$$
> $$\text{NPV} = \text{Initial Investment} + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1 + r)^t}$$

Where:
- **CF_t** = Cash flow in year *t* (negative for outflows, positive for inflows)
- **r** = Discount rate (cost of capital, hurdle rate)
- **n** = Project life in years

**Decision Rule:**
- NPV > 0 → **ACCEPT** (project adds value)
- NPV = 0 → Indifferent
- NPV < 0 → **REJECT**

### 2.3.2 Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula (Definition-based):**
> $$\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1 + \text{IRR})^t} = 0$$

**Decision Rule:**
- IRR > Cost of Capital → **ACCEPT**
- IRR < Cost of Capital → **REJECT**

### 2.3.3 Payback Period (PBP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula:**
> $$\text{PBP} = \text{Years before full recovery} + \frac{\text{Unrecovered amount at start of year}}{\text{Cash flow during the year}}$$

**Decision Rule:** PBP should be less than the organization's *maximum acceptable payback period*.

### 2.3.4 Return on Investment (ROI)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula:**
> $$\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100\%$$
> $$\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{Total Discounted Benefits} - \text{Total Discounted Costs}}{\text{Total Discounted Costs}} \times 100\%$$

### 2.3.5 Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula:**
> $$\text{BCR} = \frac{\text{Present Value of Benefits}}{\text{Present Value of Costs}}$$

**Decision Rule:**
- BCR > 1 → **ACCEPT**
- BCR < 1 → **REJECT**

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Master Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Purpose / When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PERT Expected Estimate | $E = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}$ | Cost estimation under uncertainty |
| 2 | PERT Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \frac{P - O}{6}$ | Risk quantification |
| 3 | COCOMO Effort | $E = a \cdot (\text{KLOC})^b$ | Software effort estimation |
| 4 | COCOMO Duration | $D = c \cdot (E)^d$ | Schedule estimation |
| 5 | COCOMO Staff | $N = \frac{E}{D}$ | Team sizing |
| 6 | NPV | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Financial viability |
| 7 | IRR | $\sum \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+\text{IRR})^t} = 0$ | Discount rate break-even |
| 8 | Payback Period | $\text{Years} + \frac{\text{Unrecovered}}{\text{CF}}$ | Risk-recovery time |
| 9 | ROI | $\frac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100\%$ | Efficiency of investment |
| 10 | BCR | $\frac{\text{PV of Benefits}}{\text{PV of Costs}}$ | Project worthiness |
| 11 | Discount Factor | $\frac{1}{(1+r)^t}$ | Time-value-of-money conversion |
| 12 | Annuity Present Value | $\text{APV} = \text{PMT} \cdot \frac{1-(1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ | Equal annual cash flows |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **NPV** is used by *every Fortune 500 CIO* before approving multi-crore IT projects.
- **IRR** is the language of *venture capitalists* evaluating startup pitches.
- **Payback Period** is the favourite of *SMEs* that cannot afford long recovery horizons.
- **Three-point estimation** is used by *NASA, ISRO, and DRDO* for mission-critical software.
- **COCOMO** remains the *de-facto standard* taught globally for software effort estimation.
- **BCR** is mandated by the *World Bank* and *ADB* for public-sector project sanctions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Payback Period

> **Problem Statement:**
> A software company invests ₹10,00,000 in a project. The expected cash inflows are:
> - Year 1: ₹3,00,000
> - Year 2: ₹4,00,000
> - Year 3: ₹5,00,000
> - Year 4: ₹3,00,000
>
> **Calculate the Payback Period.**

### Step 1: Compute cumulative cash flows year by year

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cumulative Cash Flow (Year 1)} &= 3{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Cumulative Cash Flow (Year 2)} &= 3{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = 7{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Cumulative Cash Flow (Year 3)} &= 7{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 = 12{,}00{,}000 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Cumulative cash flow tracks how much of the initial investment has been recovered up to that year.

### Step 2: Identify the year of full recovery

The initial investment of ₹10,00,000 lies between ₹7,00,000 (end of Year 2) and ₹12,00,000 (end of Year 3). Therefore, full recovery occurs during **Year 3**.

### Step 3: Apply the Payback Period formula

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PBP} &= \text{Years before full recovery} + \frac{\text{Unrecovered amount at start of that year}}{\text{Cash flow during that year}} \\
\text{PBP} &= 2 + \frac{10{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \\
\text{PBP} &= 2 + \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \\
\text{PBP} &= 2 + 0.6 \\
\text{PBP} &= 2.6 \text{ years} \\
\end{aligned}$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{\text{Payback Period} = 2.6 \text{ years}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The project recovers its entire initial investment in 2 years and 7.2 months. If the firm's maximum acceptable payback is 3 years, the project is **accepted**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — NPV

> **Problem Statement:**
> A project requires an initial investment of ₹5,00,000. The discount rate is 10%. The expected cash inflows are:
> - Year 1: ₹2,00,000
> - Year 2: ₹2,50,000
> - Year 3: ₹2,00,000
>
> **Compute the NPV and decide whether to invest.**

### Step 1: Set up the NPV formula

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= \text{CF}_0 + \frac{\text{CF}_1}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{\text{CF}_2}{(1+r)^2} + \frac{\text{CF}_3}{(1+r)^3} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion Logic:** CF₀ is the initial outflow (negative), CF₁..CFₙ are the future inflows that must be discounted back to *present value* because *a rupee tomorrow is worth less than a rupee today*.

### Step 2: Compute the discount factors

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DF}_1 &= \frac{1}{(1.10)^1} = 0.9091 \\
\text{DF}_2 &= \frac{1}{(1.10)^2} = 0.8264 \\
\text{DF}_3 &= \frac{1}{(1.10)^3} = 0.7513 \\
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Compute the present value of each cash flow

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PV}_1 &= 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.9091 = 1{,}81{,}820 \\
\text{PV}_2 &= 2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.8264 = 2{,}06{,}600 \\
\text{PV}_3 &= 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7513 = 1{,}50{,}260 \\
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Sum all present values

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}81{,}820 + 2{,}06{,}600 + 1{,}50{,}260 \\
\text{NPV} &= -5{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}38{,}680 \\
\text{NPV} &= +38{,}680 \\
\end{aligned}$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{\text{NPV} = ₹38{,}680 > 0 \Rightarrow \text{ACCEPT the project}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** Even when NPV is small but positive, the project is accepted. A positive NPV means the project's return exceeds the cost of capital (10%), thereby *creating value* for the firm.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Three-Point (PERT) Cost Estimation

> **Problem Statement:**
> For a software module, the project manager collects the following estimates from three experts:
> - Optimistic (O) = 40 person-days
> - Most Likely (M) = 60 person-days
> - Pessimistic (P) = 100 person-days
>
> **Compute the Expected Estimate (E), Standard Deviation, and the 95% Confidence Interval.**

### Step 1: Apply the PERT expected estimate formula

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{O + 4M + P}{6} \\
E &= \frac{40 + 4(60) + 100}{6} \\
E &= \frac{40 + 240 + 100}{6} \\
E &= \frac{380}{6} \\
E &= 63.33 \text{ person-days} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The PERT formula weights the *most likely* estimate four times because it is statistically the most probable outcome, while O and P are tail events.

### Step 2: Compute the standard deviation

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{P - O}{6} \\
\sigma &= \frac{100 - 40}{6} \\
\sigma &= \frac{60}{6} \\
\sigma &= 10 \text{ person-days} \\
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Compute the 95% confidence interval (Mean ± 2σ)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Lower Bound} &= E - 2\sigma = 63.33 - 20 = 43.33 \\
\text{Upper Bound} &= E + 2\sigma = 63.33 + 20 = 83.33 \\
\end{aligned}$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{E = 63.33 \text{ days}, \quad \sigma = 10 \text{ days}, \quad 95\% \text{ CI} = [43.33, 83.33] \text{ days}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — IRR using Trial-and-Error (Interpolation)

> **Problem Statement:**
> Initial investment = ₹1,00,000. Single cash inflow of ₹1,21,000 at the end of Year 2.
> **Find the IRR.**

### Step 1: Set up the IRR equation

$$\begin{aligned}
-1{,}00{,}000 + \frac{1{,}21{,}000}{(1 + \text{IRR})^2} &= 0 \\
\frac{1{,}21{,}000}{(1 + \text{IRR})^2} &= 1{,}00{,}000 \\
(1 + \text{IRR})^2 &= \frac{1{,}21{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = 1.21 \\
1 + \text{IRR} &= \sqrt{1.21} = 1.10 \\
\text{IRR} &= 0.10 = 10\% \\
\end{aligned}$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{\text{IRR} = 10\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision:** If cost of capital < 10% → ACCEPT; otherwise REJECT.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — ROI and BCR

> **Problem Statement:**
> - Initial Investment: ₹4,00,000
> - Annual Cash Inflow: ₹1,50,000 for 3 years
> - Salvage Value: ₹50,000 at end of Year 3
> - Discount Rate: 10%
>
> **Compute ROI and BCR.**

### Step 1: Compute present value of total benefits

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{PV of Inflows} &= 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.9091 + 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.8264 + 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.7513 + 50{,}000 \times 0.7513 \\
\text{PV of Inflows} &= 1{,}36{,}365 + 1{,}23{,}960 + 1{,}12{,}695 + 37{,}565 \\
\text{PV of Inflows} &= 4{,}10{,}585 \\
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Compute ROI

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ROI} &= \frac{\text{PV of Benefits} - \text{PV of Costs}}{\text{PV of Costs}} \times 100\% \\
\text{ROI} &= \frac{4{,}10{,}585 - 4{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% \\
\text{ROI} &= \frac{10{,}585}{4{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% \\
\text{ROI} &= 2.65\% \\
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Compute BCR

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{BCR} &= \frac{\text{PV of Benefits}}{\text{PV of Costs}} = \frac{4{,}10{,}585}{4{,}00{,}000} \\
\text{BCR} &= 1.0265 \\
\end{aligned}$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{\text{ROI} = 2.65\%, \quad \text{BCR} = 1.03}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision:** BCR > 1 ⇒ ACCEPT. The project is marginally viable; the firm must compare with alternative opportunities.

---

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Financial Appraisal Tools

| Criterion | NPV | IRR | PBP | ROI | BCR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Considers time value of money | Yes | Yes | No | Sometimes | Yes |
| Gives absolute or relative value | Absolute | Relative | Absolute | Relative | Relative |
| Handles multiple/sign-changing CFs | Yes | Complex | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Ease of computation | Medium | Hard | Easy | Easy | Easy |
| Preferred by KTU exams | Highest | High | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| Mutually exclusive projects | Reliable | Can mislead | Unreliable | Can mislead | Unreliable |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When projects are *mutually exclusive* and of different sizes/durations, **always prefer NPV** over IRR because IRR can give misleading rankings due to the reinvestment-rate assumption.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Feasibility Study Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Business Problem] --> B[Define Project Scope and Objectives]
    B --> C[Conduct Preliminary Investigation]
    C --> D{Technical Feasibility}
    C --> E{Operational Feasibility}
    C --> F{Economic Feasibility}
    C --> G{Legal Feasibility}
    C --> H{Schedule Feasibility}
    D --> I[Compile Feasibility Report]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Go / No-Go Decision}
    J -->|Go| K[Approve Project and Allocate Budget]
    J -->|NoGo| L[Archive or Re-evaluate Project]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> The feasibility study branches into **five parallel investigative streams** (Technical, Operational, Economic, Legal, Schedule). Each stream produces its own evidence-based conclusion. All five converge into the *Feasibility Report*, which the sponsor uses to make the binary *Go / No-Go* decision.

---

## 4.2 Project Identification and Selection Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Strategic Plan] --> B[Brainstorm Project Ideas]
    B --> C[Identify Candidate Projects]
    C --> D[Screen Against Strategic Alignment]
    D --> E[Market and Demand Analysis]
    E --> F[Preliminary Cost Estimate]
    F --> G[Financial Appraisal NPV IRR BCR]
    G --> H{Rank and Prioritize}
    H --> I[Shortlist for Feasibility Study]
    I --> J[Approved Project Pipeline]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> Project identification is *not random ideation*. It must flow from the **organizational strategic plan**, be filtered through market analysis, cost estimation, and financial appraisal, and only the most promising candidates proceed to the detailed feasibility study.

---

## 4.3 Cost Estimation Hierarchy (from Rough to Precise)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Order of Magnitude Estimate] -->|Accuracy -50 percent to +50 percent| B[Budget Estimate]
    B -->|Accuracy -30 percent to +30 percent| C[Definitive Estimate]
    C -->|Accuracy -10 percent to +10 percent| D[Final Detailed Estimate]
    A1[Analogous Technique] -.-> A
    B1[Parametric Technique] -.-> B
    C1[Bottoms Up WBS Technique] -.-> C
    D1[Three Point PERT and COCOMO] -.-> D
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> Estimates evolve from *rough* (Order of Magnitude — used at concept stage) to *precise* (Final Detailed — used at execution stage). Each refinement requires more data, more time, and more cost — but yields higher accuracy.

---

## 4.4 Financial Appraisal Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compile Cash Flow Forecasts] --> B[Apply Discount Rate r]
    B --> C[Compute NPV]
    C --> D{NPV > 0?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Cross-Check with IRR]
    D -->|No| F[REJECT Project]
    E --> G{IRR > r?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Cross-Check PBP and BCR]
    G -->|No| F
    H --> I{PBP acceptable and BCR > 1?}
    I -->|Yes| J[ACCEPT Project]
    I -->|No| K[Re-negotiate Scope or Budget]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> A rigorous financial appraisal uses **multiple cross-checks**. A project passing all four filters (NPV, IRR, PBP, BCR) is robustly accepted. Passing only one or two is a *red flag* that warrants scope re-negotiation.

---

## 4.5 Inter-relationship of Feasibility Pillars

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INDEPENDENT["Independent Pillars"]
    T[Technical Feasibility]
    O[Operational Feasibility]
    L[Legal Feasibility]
    S[Schedule Feasibility]
    end
    subgraph DERIVED["Derived from Cost and Revenue"]
    E[Economic Feasibility]
    end
    CE[Project Cost Estimate] --> E
    MA[Market and Demand Analysis] --> E
    FA[Financial Appraisal] --> E
    T --> FS[Feasibility Study Output]
    O --> FS
    L --> FS
    S --> FS
    E --> FS
    FS --> DECISION[Go or No-Go Decision]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> Four pillars (Technical, Operational, Legal, Schedule) are *qualitative*. The fifth pillar (Economic) is *quantitative* and is derived from cost estimation and market analysis. Together, the five pillars form the complete Feasibility Study output that drives the final Go/No-Go decision.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the concept of a Feasibility Study. List and briefly explain any three types of feasibility studies.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Feasibility Study** is a preliminary investigation carried out to assess whether a proposed project is *viable* across multiple dimensions before committing significant organizational resources. It acts as the *gateway* between project identification and project initiation.

Three major types:

1. **Technical Feasibility** — Evaluates whether the proposed system can be built using *available technology, infrastructure, and skilled personnel*. Example: Checking if a face-recognition attendance system has the required camera hardware and AI libraries.

2. **Operational Feasibility** — Determines whether the system will be *accepted and effectively used* by end-users and whether the organization has the *culture and processes* to support it. Example: Whether hospital staff will adopt an electronic health record system.

3. **Economic / Financial Feasibility** — Analyses *cost-benefit* using NPV, IRR, payback period, ROI, and BCR to verify the project is *financially justifiable*.

> [!TIP]
> **Marks Distribution (as per KTU pattern):**
> - Definition (1 mark)
> - Any three types with explanation (2 marks — 1 mark each)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between Project Identification and Project Screening.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Project Identification | Project Screening |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | To generate and recognize potential project ideas | To filter and shortlist viable projects from the pool |
| **Stage** | Idea-generation phase | Idea-evaluation phase |
| **Input** | Strategic plans, market gaps, customer needs | Identified project ideas |
| **Output** | List of candidate projects | Shortlist of priority projects |
| **Tools Used** | Brainstorming, SWOT, Delphi technique | Scoring models, NPV, strategic alignment |
| **Frequency** | Continuous / periodic | After identification cycle |

> **Conclusion:** Identification answers *“What projects could we do?”* while Screening answers *“Which of these should we do?”*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five pillars of a Feasibility Study** with suitable examples for a software project. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A company is evaluating two mutually exclusive software projects. Both require an initial investment of ₹5,00,000. The discount rate is 12%. The expected cash inflows are:

| Year | Project A (₹) | Project B (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2,00,000 | 1,00,000 |
| 2 | 2,00,000 | 2,00,000 |
| 3 | 2,00,000 | 3,00,000 |
| 4 | 1,50,000 | 3,00,000 |

**Compute the NPV of both projects and recommend which one to choose.** *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

The **five pillars of a Feasibility Study** are:

**1. Technical Feasibility:**
   - Evaluates whether the proposed software system can be developed using available or acquirable technology.
   - Checks hardware capacity, software platforms, network bandwidth, and skill availability.
   - *Example:* For a real-time stock trading platform, technical feasibility checks if the team can build a low-latency (< 50 ms) matching engine using available programming languages (Java/C++), messaging middleware (Kafka), and cloud infrastructure (AWS).

**2. Operational Feasibility:**
   - Assesses whether the system will be accepted by end-users and integrated smoothly into existing workflows.
   - Examines organizational culture, training needs, change-management burden, and user attitude.
   - *Example:* For a university ERP system, operational feasibility checks if faculty will adapt to digital attendance and if students will accept online fee payment.

**3. Economic / Financial Feasibility:**
   - Quantifies whether the project is financially worthwhile.
   - Uses NPV, IRR, ROI, PBP, BCR.
   - *Example:* Computing the NPV of a CRM implementation costing ₹50 Lakhs with projected annual savings of ₹15 Lakhs over 5 years at a discount rate of 12%.

**4. Legal Feasibility:**
   - Ensures compliance with laws, regulations, IP rights, and contractual obligations.
   - *Example:* Verifying that the AI-based recruitment tool complies with the DPDP Act 2023 and is free from gender-bias violations.

**5. Schedule Feasibility:**
   - Confirms the project can be completed within the required timeline.
   - Examines resource calendars, dependencies, and critical path.
   - *Example:* A government portal must launch before March 31 (financial year-end); schedule feasibility checks if 6 months are sufficient given vendor delivery timelines.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Step 1: Compute Discount Factors at r = 12%**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DF}_1 &= \frac{1}{1.12} = 0.8929 \\
\text{DF}_2 &= \frac{1}{(1.12)^2} = 0.7972 \\
\text{DF}_3 &= \frac{1}{(1.12)^3} = 0.7118 \\
\text{DF}_4 &= \frac{1}{(1.12)^4} = 0.6355 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute NPV of Project A**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}_A &= -5{,}00{,}000 + (2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8929) + (2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7972) + (2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7118) + (1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.6355) \\
\text{NPV}_A &= -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}78{,}580 + 1{,}59{,}440 + 1{,}42{,}360 + 95{,}325 \\
\text{NPV}_A &= -5{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}75{,}705 \\
\text{NPV}_A &= ₹75{,}705 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute NPV of Project B**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV}_B &= -5{,}00{,}000 + (1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8929) + (2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7972) + (3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7118) + (3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6355) \\
\text{NPV}_B &= -5{,}00{,}000 + 89{,}290 + 1{,}59{,}440 + 2{,}13{,}540 + 1{,}90{,}650 \\
\text{NPV}_B &= -5{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}52{,}920 \\
\text{NPV}_B &= ₹1{,}52{,}920 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Recommendation**

$$\boxed{\text{NPV}_B = ₹1{,}52{,}920 > \text{NPV}_A = ₹75{,}705 \Rightarrow \text{Choose Project B}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion Logic:** Although Project A gives earlier cash flows (more front-loaded), Project B's larger later cash flows dominate when discounted. **NPV rule for mutually exclusive projects: Always choose the project with the highest NPV.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **three-point estimation (PERT) technique** for project cost estimation. Compute the expected estimate, standard deviation, and 95% confidence interval for the following module-level estimates:

| Module | O (days) | M (days) | P (days) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Login | 5 | 8 | 15 |
| Payment | 8 | 12 | 25 |
| Reports | 10 | 14 | 30 |

**Estimate the total project effort and its 95% confidence range.** *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Market and Demand Analysis**. Discuss the **TAM-SAM-SOM framework** with a suitable example. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Three-Point Estimation (PERT) — Theory:**
The PERT technique uses three estimates — **Optimistic (O), Most Likely (M), and Pessimistic (P)** — to compute a *weighted average* expected estimate. It accounts for the *Beta distribution* of real-world estimates and quantifies uncertainty through standard deviation.

**Step 1: Compute Expected Estimate for each module**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{Login}} &= \frac{5 + 4(8) + 15}{6} = \frac{5 + 32 + 15}{6} = \frac{52}{6} = 8.67 \text{ days} \\
E_{\text{Payment}} &= \frac{8 + 4(12) + 25}{6} = \frac{8 + 48 + 25}{6} = \frac{81}{6} = 13.50 \text{ days} \\
E_{\text{Reports}} &= \frac{10 + 4(14) + 30}{6} = \frac{10 + 56 + 30}{6} = \frac{96}{6} = 16.00 \text{ days} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute Standard Deviation for each module**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{Login}} &= \frac{15 - 5}{6} = \frac{10}{6} = 1.67 \\
\sigma_{\text{Payment}} &= \frac{25 - 8}{6} = \frac{17}{6} = 2.83 \\
\sigma_{\text{Reports}} &= \frac{30 - 10}{6} = \frac{20}{6} = 3.33 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute Total Project Expected Estimate**

$$E_{\text{Total}} = 8.67 + 13.50 + 16.00 = 38.17 \text{ days}$$

**Step 4: Compute Combined Variance (sum of individual variances)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2_{\text{Total}} &= (1.67)^2 + (2.83)^2 + (3.33)^2 \\
&= 2.79 + 8.01 + 11.09 \\
&= 21.89 \\
\sigma_{\text{Total}} &= \sqrt{21.89} = 4.68 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: 95% Confidence Interval (E ± 2σ)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Lower Bound} &= 38.17 - 2(4.68) = 38.17 - 9.36 = 28.81 \text{ days} \\
\text{Upper Bound} &= 38.17 + 2(4.68) = 38.17 + 9.36 = 47.53 \text{ days} \\
\end{aligned}$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{E_{\text{Total}} = 38.17 \text{ days}, \quad \sigma_{\text{Total}} = 4.68 \text{ days}, \quad 95\% \text{ CI} = [28.81, 47.53] \text{ days}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic for Combined Variance:** When modules are *independent*, the total variance is the **sum** of individual variances (not the sum of standard deviations). This is a frequently-tested KTU concept.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Market and Demand Analysis — Definition:**
**Market and Demand Analysis** is a systematic study of the *existing market structure, customer segments, competitive landscape, and projected demand* for a proposed product or service. It determines the *revenue potential* and *commercial viability* of the project.

**Objectives:**
1. To estimate the total demand for the proposed product/service.
2. To identify the target customer segments.
3. To analyze competitors and substitute products.
4. To forecast future demand trends.
5. To determine market share achievable by the project.

**TAM-SAM-SOM Framework:**

| Level | Full Form | Meaning | Example (Online Education Platform in India) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TAM** | Total Addressable Market | Entire global revenue opportunity | All education spending in India = ₹10,00,000 Crore |
| **SAM** | Serviceable Available Market | The segment you can target | K-12 and Higher Ed online segment = ₹50,000 Crore |
| **SOM** | Serviceable Obtainable Market | The share you can realistically capture | 2% of SAM in 3 years = ₹1,000 Crore |

**Methods of Demand Forecasting:**

| Method | Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Survey-based | Qualitative | Customer interviews, Delphi |
| Trend Analysis | Quantitative | Linear regression on past sales |
| Moving Average | Quantitative | 3-year moving average of demand |
| Exponential Smoothing | Quantitative | Weighted recent data more heavily |
| Econometric Models | Quantitative | Multi-variable regression |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Always tie the *Market and Demand Analysis* output back to *revenue projections* in the financial appraisal. A market without quantified demand is just a guess, not an analysis.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in This Topic (Avoid at All Costs):**
>
> 1. **Confusing NPV with Profit:** NPV is *value created* in present-value terms, NOT accounting profit. Many students subtract costs from revenues without discounting — full marks lost.
>
> 2. **Wrong variance aggregation in PERT:** Students add standard deviations instead of variances. **Always sum variances** (σ²), then take the square root for combined σ.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the initial investment sign:** CF₀ is *negative* (outflow). Forgetting the minus sign yields a wrong NPV sign.
>
> 4. **Treating Payback Period as time-value-aware:** Payback Period uses *undiscounted* cash flows. If the question says "discounted payback", you MUST use discounted CF.
>
> 5. **Mis-stating COCOMO project types:** Organic / Semi-detached / Embedded have different constants. Memorize the table.
>
> 6. **Skipping TAM-SAM-SOM definitions:** KTU examiners frequently award 1–2 marks for *exact definitions* of these three terms.
>
> 7. **Writing "feasibility study" without specifying which type:** Always state which pillar — Technical, Operational, Economic, Legal, or Schedule.
>
> 8. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** Always write "₹", "person-days", "%", or "years" — losing these drops 0.5 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Rapid-Fire Revision Bullets (Save for last-day revision):**

### A. Conceptual Anchors
- ✅ A **Project** is *temporary and unique* — distinguish from *operations*.
- ✅ A **Feasibility Study** is the *Go/No-Go* gate before project sanction.
- ✅ The **Five Pillars** are: **T.O.E.L.S.** (Technical, Operational, Economic, Legal, Schedule).
- ✅ **Project Identification** generates ideas; **Project Screening** filters them.
- ✅ **TAM → SAM → SOM** is a funnel — each level is smaller than the previous.
- ✅ **Cost estimation** techniques in order of accuracy: Analogous → Parametric → Bottom-Up → Three-Point/COCOMO.

### B. Formula Anchors (Must memorize)
- ✅ PERT: $E = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}$, $\sigma = \frac{P - O}{6}$, total variance = sum of variances.
- ✅ COCOMO: $E = a \cdot (\text{KLOC})^b$, $D = c \cdot E^d$, $N = E / D$.
- ✅ NPV: $\sum \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+r)^t}$; Accept if NPV > 0.
- ✅ IRR: $\sum \frac{\text{CF}_t}{(1+\text{IRR})^t} = 0$; Accept if IRR > r.
- ✅ PBP: Years + (Unrecovered ÷ CF during year).
- ✅ ROI: $\frac{\text{Benefit} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100\%$.
- ✅ BCR: $\frac{\text{PV Benefits}}{\text{PV Costs}}$; Accept if BCR > 1.

### C. Decision Rules (Must memorize)
- ✅ NPV > 0 → ACCEPT.
- ✅ IRR > Cost of Capital → ACCEPT.
- ✅ BCR > 1 → ACCEPT.
- ✅ Payback < Max Acceptable → ACCEPT.
- ✅ For *mutually exclusive* projects → choose highest NPV (not IRR).

### D. Common Numerical Patterns
- ✅ Cumulative cash flow table for PBP.
- ✅ Discount factor table at given r.
- ✅ Multi-row cash-flow NPV with 4–5 years.
- ✅ Three-point PERT with 3 modules and combined variance.

### E. Vocabulary Bank (Use exact KTU terminology in answers)
- Temporary endeavour, progressive elaboration, scope, stakeholder.
- Order-of-magnitude estimate, definitive estimate, contingency reserve.
- Sunk cost, opportunity cost, discounted cash flow, hurdle rate.
- Discount rate, cost of capital, salvage value, terminal value.
- Beta distribution, confidence interval, sensitivity analysis.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST521 - SOFTWARE PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Project Scheduling: Introduction to PERT and CPM, Critical Path Calculation, Precedence Relationship, Difference Between PERT and CPM, Float Calculation and Its Importance, Cost Reduction by Crashing of Activity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Project Scheduling?

**Project Scheduling** is the process of defining, planning, sequencing, and controlling the execution of project activities to achieve defined objectives within a stipulated time and budget. In the context of Software Project Management, scheduling defines *what work will be done, in what order, by whom, and by when*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Project Scheduling is the phase of project planning that determines the logical order of activities, estimates their durations, identifies the critical path, and creates a baseline calendar for monitoring and controlling project execution.*

## 1.2 Introduction to PERT and CPM

### PERT — Program Evaluation and Review Technique

PERT is a **probabilistic (stochastic) project management technique** developed in **1958** by the U.S. Navy (Bo Polaris Missile Program) for managing complex, R&D-heavy projects where activity durations are uncertain. It uses **three time estimates** (optimistic, most likely, pessimistic) and computes an **expected time** for each activity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Idea of PERT:** Focus is on **TIME**. Duration of activities is uncertain, modelled using a Beta Probability Distribution. The formula is:
> $$t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$$
> where $t_o$ = Optimistic, $t_m$ = Most Likely, $t_p$ = Pessimistic.

### CPM — Critical Path Method

CPM is a **deterministic project management technique** developed in **1957** by DuPont and Remington Rand for industrial engineering and chemical plant projects. It uses a **single, fixed time estimate** for each activity, and its emphasis is on balancing **Time and Cost Trade-off** (Crashing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Idea of CPM:** Focus is on **TIME + COST**. Activity durations are deterministic. The technique identifies the **Critical Path** — the longest duration path that determines the minimum project completion time.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Wedding Planner

> [!TIP]
> **Think of PERT/CPM as Planning a Big Fat Indian Wedding 💍:**
> - **Activities** = booking venue, sending cards, catering, pandit, music, etc.
> - **Precedence Relationships** = you cannot "send invitation cards" before "finalize guest list", and you cannot "cater food" before "decide menu".
> - **Network Diagram** = the wedding planner's Gantt chart on the wall.
> - **Critical Path** = the *longest chain of must-do tasks* (e.g., Venue → Pandit → Muhurtham) — any delay here delays the entire wedding.
> - **Float** = how much you can delay the "music arrangement" without delaying the wedding (slack time).
> - **Crashing** = paying extra for a same-day premium caterer to compress the schedule by 1 day.

If the longest chain (Critical Path) takes **30 days**, then *no matter how fast you arrange flowers*, the wedding cannot happen in less than 30 days. That is the essence of project scheduling.

## 1.3 Key Terms at a Glance

| Term | Meaning | Notation |
|------|---------|----------|
| **Activity** | A task consuming time and resources | Arrow ( → ) |
| **Event / Node** | A milestone — start or finish of an activity | Circle ( ○ ) |
| **Dummy Activity** | A logical dependency with zero duration | Dashed arrow ( --→ ) |
| **Predecessor** | Activity that must finish before another starts | — |
| **Successor** | Activity that follows another | — |
| **Critical Path** | Longest path; zero total float | CP |
| **Float / Slack** | Time an activity can be delayed | $T_F$, $F_F$ |
| **EST** | Earliest Start Time | $E_j$ for node $j$ |
| **LFT** | Latest Finish Time | $L_j$ for node $j$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Network Construction — AON vs AOA

There are two ways to draw project networks:

| Type | Full Form | Convention Used By |
|------|-----------|-------------------|
| **AOA** | Activity on Arrow | Classical PERT/CPM |
| **AON** | Activity on Node | Modern PM software (MS Project, Primavera) |

KTU questions generally expect **AOA (Arrow-on-Arrow) networks**, where each arrow represents an activity and nodes represent events.

## 2.2 Forward Pass — Computing Earliest Times (EET)

We walk **left → right** through the network, calculating the **Earliest Event Time ($E_j$)** at each node $j$.

**Rule:** The earliest event time at a node equals the **maximum** of the earliest event time of its predecessor plus the duration of the activity connecting them.

$$E_j = \max_{i \in P(j)} \left[ E_i + t_{ij} \right]$$

For the start node: $E_1 = 0$.

## 2.3 Backward Pass — Computing Latest Times (LET)

We walk **right → left** through the network, calculating the **Latest Event Time ($L_i$)** at each node $i$.

**Rule:** The latest event time at a node equals the **minimum** of the latest event time of its successor minus the duration of the activity connecting them.

$$L_i = \min_{j \in S(i)} \left[ L_j - t_{ij} \right]$$

For the end node: $L_n = E_n$ (project duration).

## 2.4 Float Calculations — The Heart of CPM

### 2.4.1 Total Float ($TF$)

The amount of time an activity can be delayed **without delaying the project completion date**.

$$TF_{ij} = L_j - E_i - t_{ij}$$

### 2.4.2 Free Float ($FF$)

The amount of time an activity can be delayed **without delaying the early start of any successor activity**.

$$FF_{ij} = E_j - E_i - t_{ij}$$

### 2.4.3 Independent Float ($IF$)

The amount of time an activity can be delayed **when its predecessor is at its latest start AND its successor is at its earliest start**.

$$IF_{ij} = E_j - L_i - t_{ij} \quad \text{(reported as 0 if negative)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Relationship:** $FF \ge IF$ and $TF \ge FF \ge IF$.
> **Critical Activity:** Any activity with $TF = 0$. All critical activities form the **Critical Path**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference

| # | Formula | Use |
|---|---------|-----|
| 1 | $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ | PERT expected time (Beta distribution) |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}$ | PERT standard deviation per activity |
| 3 | $\sigma_{proj} = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_{cp}^2}$ | Project standard deviation along critical path |
| 4 | $E_j = \max \left[ E_i + t_{ij} \right]$ | Earliest event time (Forward pass) |
| 5 | $L_i = \min \left[ L_j - t_{ij} \right]$ | Latest event time (Backward pass) |
| 6 | $TF_{ij} = L_j - E_i - t_{ij}$ | Total Float |
| 7 | $FF_{ij} = E_j - E_i - t_{ij}$ | Free Float |
| 8 | $IF_{ij} = E_j - L_i - t_{ij}$ | Independent Float |
| 9 | $CS = \dfrac{C_c - C_n}{T_n - T_c}$ | Cost Slope for Crashing |
| 10 | $TC = DC + IC$ | Total Project Cost (Direct + Indirect) |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Tip:** In every KTU numerical, after computing $E_j$ and $L_i$ for all nodes, always tabulate $ES$, $EF$, $LS$, $LF$, $TF$ and $FF$ for each activity. The row(s) with $TF=0$ form the Critical Path.

## 2.6 Crashing — Cost Reduction by Activity Compression

**Crashing** is the process of shortening the project duration by adding extra resources (overtime, more manpower) to critical activities, at an additional cost called the **Crash Cost**.

- **Normal Time ($T_n$) / Normal Cost ($C_n$)** — duration and cost under normal working conditions.
- **Crash Time ($T_c$) / Crash Cost ($C_c$)** — minimum achievable duration and its cost.
- **Cost Slope ($CS$)** — additional cost per unit of time saved.

$$CS = \dfrac{C_c - C_n}{T_n - T_c}$$

**The Crashing Logic (Step-by-Step):**

1. Compute the normal critical path and duration.
2. Calculate the cost slope of **every critical activity**.
3. Pick the activity with the **lowest cost slope** on the critical path.
4. Crash it by **1 day** (or to the limit where another path becomes critical).
5. If multiple paths become critical, crash a combination that reduces **all** critical paths.
6. Stop when the crashing cost per day **exceeds the indirect cost saved per day**, or all activities reach their crash limits.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When multiple paths become critical, students often forget that you must crash activities to shorten **every** critical path simultaneously. The cost of crashing is then the **sum of cost slopes** of the selected activities.

## 2.7 PERT vs CPM — Master Comparison Table

| Parameter | PERT | CPM |
|-----------|------|-----|
| **Stands for** | Program Evaluation and Review Technique | Critical Path Method |
| **Year & Origin** | 1958, U.S. Navy (Bo Polaris) | 1957, DuPont & Remington Rand |
| **Time Estimates** | 3 (Optimistic, Most Likely, Pessimistic) | 1 (Single deterministic estimate) |
| **Approach** | Probabilistic / Stochastic | Deterministic |
| **Focus** | Time | Time and Cost |
| **Distribution Used** | Beta Distribution | None (single estimate) |
| **Network Type** | Activity on Arrow (AOA) | Activity on Arrow (AOA) |
| **Best Suited For** | R&D, Research, Software dev. prototypes | Construction, Manufacturing, Repetitive projects |
| **Crashing** | Not primarily used | Primary use case |
| **Dummy Activities** | Often required | Less frequent |
| **Software Project Suitability** | High (uncertain requirements) | Moderate (well-defined scope) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Critical Path & Float Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**

A software project consists of the following activities. Construct the network, find the critical path, project duration, and the total/float of each activity.

| Activity | Predecessor | Duration (days) |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| A | — | 3 |
| B | — | 4 |
| C | A | 2 |
| D | A | 5 |
| E | B, C | 6 |
| F | B | 4 |
| G | D, E | 3 |

### Step 1 — Identify Logical Relationships

- A and B start at node 1 (no predecessor).
- C and D depend only on A.
- E depends on B and C (so C must finish, then B must finish, then E can start).
- F depends only on B.
- G depends on D and E.

We will need a **dummy activity** from C's end to merge B and C into E.

### Step 2 — Construct the Network (AOA)

Nodes: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7

- Node 1 → Node 2 : A (3)
- Node 1 → Node 3 : B (4)
- Node 2 → Node 4 : C (2)
- Node 2 → Node 5 : D (5)
- Node 4 → Node 6 : Dummy (0)
- Node 3 → Node 6 : Dummy (0)
- Node 6 → Node 7 : E (6)  *(E starts only after B and C both finish)*
- Node 3 → Node 7 : F (4)    *(F starts after B)*
- Node 5 → Node 7 : Dummy (0)
- Node 7 → End (8) : G (3)   *(G starts after D and E)*

### Step 3 — Forward Pass (Earliest Event Times $E_j$)

Applying $E_j = \max(E_i + t_{ij})$:

$$E_1 = 0$$

$$E_2 = E_1 + t_A = 0 + 3 = 3$$

$$E_3 = E_1 + t_B = 0 + 4 = 4$$

$$E_4 = E_2 + t_C = 3 + 2 = 5$$

$$E_5 = E_2 + t_D = 3 + 5 = 8$$

$$E_6 = \max(E_3 + 0,\; E_4 + 0) = \max(4, 5) = 5$$

$$E_7 = \max(E_6 + t_E,\; E_3 + t_F,\; E_5 + 0) = \max(5+6,\; 4+4,\; 8+0) = \max(11,\; 8,\; 8) = 11$$

$$E_8 = E_7 + t_G = 11 + 3 = 14$$

**Project Duration = 14 days.**

### Step 4 — Backward Pass (Latest Event Times $L_i$)

For the end node, $L_8 = E_8 = 14$. Applying $L_i = \min(L_j - t_{ij})$:

$$L_7 = L_8 - t_G = 14 - 3 = 11$$

$$L_6 = L_7 - t_E = 11 - 6 = 5$$

$$L_5 = L_7 - 0 = 11 - 0 = 11$$

$$L_3 = \min(L_7 - t_F,\; L_6 - 0) = \min(11-4,\; 5-0) = \min(7,\; 5) = 5$$

$$L_4 = L_6 - 0 = 5 - 0 = 5$$

$$L_2 = \min(L_4 - t_C,\; L_5 - t_D) = \min(5-2,\; 11-5) = \min(3,\; 6) = 3$$

$$L_1 = \min(L_2 - t_A,\; L_3 - t_B) = \min(3-3,\; 5-4) = \min(0,\; 1) = 0$$

✅ $L_1 = 0$ confirms the network is consistent.

### Step 5 — Float Table for Every Activity

For each activity $(i, j)$: $ES = E_i$, $EF = E_i + t_{ij}$, $LS = L_j - t_{ij}$, $LF = L_j$, $TF = LS - ES$, $FF = E_j - EF$.

| Activity | i → j | $t_{ij}$ | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | FF |
|----------|-------|----------|----|----|----|----|----|-----|
| A | 1→2 | 3 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 | **0** ✓ | 0 |
| B | 1→3 | 4 | 0 | 4 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 0 |
| C | 2→4 | 2 | 3 | 5 | 3 | 5 | **0** ✓ | 0 |
| D | 2→5 | 5 | 3 | 8 | 6 | 11 | 3 | 0 |
| E | 6→7 | 6 | 5 | 11 | 5 | 11 | **0** ✓ | 0 |
| F | 3→7 | 4 | 4 | 8 | 7 | 11 | 3 | 3 |
| G | 7→8 | 3 | 11 | 14 | 11 | 14 | **0** ✓ | 0 |

### Step 6 — Identify the Critical Path

Activities with $TF = 0$: **A → C → E → G**

$$\boxed{\text{Critical Path: } 1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 6 \to 7 \to 8, \quad T_{project} = 14 \text{ days}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by Path Lengths:**
> - Path A–C–E–G : $3+2+6+3 = 14$ ← longest ✓
> - Path A–D–G    : $3+5+3 = 11$
> - Path B–E–G    : $4+6+3 = 13$
> - Path B–F–G    : $4+4+3 = 11$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Crashing for Cost Reduction

**Problem Statement:**

For the following network, indirect cost is **₹300 per day**. Crash the project optimally to find the minimum total cost and corresponding duration.

| Activity | Normal Time (days) | Normal Cost (₹) | Crash Time (days) | Crash Cost (₹) |
|----------|--------------------|------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1–2 (A) | 5 | 500 | 3 | 1000 |
| 1–3 (B) | 6 | 400 | 4 | 800 |
| 2–3 (C) | 2 | 300 | 1 | 500 |
| 2–4 (D) | 7 | 800 | 5 | 1200 |
| 3–4 (E) | 4 | 600 | 2 | 1000 |

### Step 1 — Compute Cost Slopes

$$CS_A = \dfrac{1000-500}{5-3} = \dfrac{500}{2} = ₹250/\text{day}$$

$$CS_B = \dfrac{800-400}{6-4} = \dfrac{400}{2} = ₹200/\text{day}$$

$$CS_C = \dfrac{500-300}{2-1} = \dfrac{200}{1} = ₹200/\text{day}$$

$$CS_D = \dfrac{1200-800}{7-5} = \dfrac{400}{2} = ₹200/\text{day}$$

$$CS_E = \dfrac{1000-600}{4-2} = \dfrac{400}{2} = ₹200/\text{day}$$

### Step 2 — Find Initial Critical Path

Possible paths:
- 1–2–3–4 (A–C–E) = $5+2+4 = 11$ days
- 1–2–4    (A–D)   = $5+7 = 12$ days  ← **Critical**
- 1–3–4    (B–E)   = $6+4 = 10$ days

**Initial CP = 1–2–4, Duration = 12 days.**

Normal total cost = $500+400+300+800+600 = ₹2600$ (direct).
Initial indirect cost = $12 \times 300 = ₹3600$.
**Total Cost = ₹6200.**

### Step 3 — Crashing Iteration 1

Critical path: A–D. Activities on CP: A (₹250/d), D (₹200/d).
**Choose D (lowest slope).** Crash D by 1 day: D goes $7 \to 6$.

- Path A–C–E: $5+2+4 = 11$
- Path A–D  : $5+6 = 11$
- Path B–E  : $6+4 = 10$

**Now both A–C–E and A–D are critical (11 days).** Indirect cost: $11 \times 300 = ₹3300$.

### Step 4 — Crashing Iteration 2

To reduce 1 day, must shorten **both** critical paths.
- A–C–E: A(250), C(200), E(200)
- A–D: A(250), D(200)

Options:
- Crash A (₹250) — shortens both paths → 1 activity, ₹250.
- Crash D (₹200) + C (₹200) or E (₹200) — 2 activities, ₹400.

**Crash A by 1 day** (cheaper, 5 → 4). New A = 4.

- A–C–E: $4+2+4 = 10$
- A–D  : $4+6 = 10$
- B–E  : $6+4 = 10$

All three paths now tie at 10 days. Duration = 10.

### Step 5 — Crashing Iteration 3

To shorten further, we must reduce **all three** paths.
- A–C–E: A(250), C(200), E(200)
- A–D  : A(250), D(200)
- B–E  : B(200), E(200)

Try E + D + B vs A + (something):
- E is common to A–C–E and B–E. If we crash E, it helps 2 paths.
- E(200) + D(200) + B(200) = ₹600 → reduces all 3.
- A(250) + D(200) + B(200) = ₹650 → reduces all 3 (since A is in first 2).
- Even cheaper: just check combinations.

**Cheapest combo = E + D + B = ₹600** (3 days, all paths down by 1).

Crash E: $4 \to 3$, D: $6 \to 5$, B: $6 \to 5$.
- A–C–E: $4+2+3 = 9$
- A–D  : $4+5 = 9$
- B–E  : $5+3 = 8$

New duration = 9 days. Indirect cost: $9 \times 300 = ₹2700$.

### Step 6 — Check Further Crashing

E and D are still within limits. Let's crash again with E + D + B:
E: $3 \to 2$ (last), D: $5 \to 4$, B: $5 \to 4$.
- A–C–E: $4+2+2 = 8$
- A–D  : $4+4 = 8$
- B–E  : $4+2 = 6$

New duration = 8. Indirect cost: $8 \times 300 = ₹2400$.

Continue: E cannot crash further (E=2 is limit). Must use D + B + A or D + B + C:
- D + B + A = 200+200+250 = ₹650
- D + B + C = 200+200+200 = ₹600 (C: $2 \to 1$, C limit reached)

Crash D, B, C: D=3, B=3, C=1.
- A–C–E: $4+1+2 = 7$
- A–D  : $4+3 = 7$
- B–E  : $3+2 = 5$

New duration = 7.

Crash one more time: D + B + C — C and D cannot be crashed more. Must use A instead:
- A(250) + B(200) + D(200) = ₹650, A: $4 \to 3$ (A limit reached).
- Now A, C, D, B at limits. Only E is non-minimal? E=2 limit too. All activities at minimum!
- **Final crashed duration = 7 days.**

### Step 7 — Tabulate the Cost Summary

| Iteration | Duration (days) | Direct Cost (₹) | Indirect Cost (₹) | Total Cost (₹) |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|--------------------|------------------|
| Initial | 12 | 2600 | 3600 | **6200** |
| 1 | 11 | 2800 | 3300 | 6100 |
| 2 | 10 | 3050 | 3000 | **6050** ← min so far |
| 3 | 9 | 3650 | 2700 | 6350 |
| 4 | 8 | 4250 | 2400 | 6650 |
| 5 | 7 | 4850 | 2100 | 6950 |

$$\boxed{\text{Optimal Project Duration} = 10 \text{ days}, \quad \text{Minimum Total Cost} = ₹6050}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Table:** As we crash beyond 10 days, the direct cost rises faster than the indirect cost falls. The optimum is where the **marginal crashing cost = indirect cost saved per day (₹300)**. Beyond 10 days, cost slope combinations exceed ₹300/day, so crashing is uneconomical.

**Distinguish between PERT and CPM. List any three points of difference.**

| S.No. | PERT | CPM |
|-------|------|-----|

| 2 | **Probabilistic** model based on Beta distribution | **Deterministic** model |

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by "Total Float" in a project network? Why is its calculation important?**

**Model Answer:**

**Total Float ($TF$)** of an activity is the amount of time by which the activity can be delayed **without delaying the overall project completion date**.

**Mathematical Definition:**
$$TF_{ij} = L_j - E_i - t_{ij}$$

where $L_j$ = latest event time of successor node, $E_i$ = earliest event time of current node, $t_{ij}$ = activity duration.

**Importance of Float Calculation:**
1. **Identifies Critical Activities** — activities with $TF = 0$ form the critical path; these need **strict monitoring**.
2. **Resource Levelling** — activities with high float can be **delayed or re-prioritised** to free up resources for critical activities.
3. **Schedule Flexibility** — managers know which activities are "tight" and which have **slack** for negotiation.
4. **Risk Management** — float acts as a **time buffer** against unforeseen delays on non-critical paths.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]**

**A small software project consists of the following activities. Construct the AOA network, find the critical path, project duration, and compute the total float and free float of each activity.**

| Activity | Predecessor(s) | Duration (weeks) |
|----------|----------------|------------------|
| A | — | 4 |
| B | — | 6 |
| C | A | 3 |
| D | A | 5 |
| E | B, C | 4 |
| F | B | 7 |
| G | D, E | 2 |

#### Part (a) — Network, Critical Path & Duration **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Construct AOA Network:**

Nodes 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8 with:
- 1→2 : A (4)
- 1→3 : B (6)
- 2→4 : C (3)
- 2→5 : D (5)
- 4→6 : Dummy (0)
- 3→6 : Dummy (0)
- 6→7 : E (4)
- 3→7 : F (7)
- 5→7 : Dummy (0)
- 7→8 : G (2)

**Step 2 — Forward Pass (EET):**

$$E_1 = 0, \quad E_2 = 0+4 = 4, \quad E_3 = 0+6 = 6, \quad E_4 = 4+3 = 7$$

$$E_5 = 4+5 = 9, \quad E_6 = \max(6+0,\; 7+0) = 7$$

$$E_7 = \max(7+4,\; 6+7,\; 9+0) = \max(11, 13, 9) = 13$$

$$E_8 = 13+2 = 15 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{T_{project} = 15 \text{ weeks}}$$

**Step 3 — Backward Pass (LET):**

$$L_8 = 15, \quad L_7 = 15-2 = 13$$

$$L_6 = 13-4 = 9, \quad L_5 = 13-0 = 13$$

$$L_3 = \min(13-7,\; 9-0) = \min(6,\; 9) = 6$$

$$L_4 = 9-0 = 9, \quad L_2 = \min(9-3,\; 13-5) = \min(6,\; 8) = 6$$

$$L_1 = \min(6-4,\; 6-6) = \min(2,\; 0) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Critical Path:**

Paths and durations:
- A–C–E–G: $4+3+4+2 = 13$
- A–D–G   : $4+5+2 = 11$
- B–E–G   : $6+4+2 = 12$
- B–F–G   : $6+7+2 = 15$ ← **Longest**

$$\boxed{\text{Critical Path: } 1 \to 3 \to 7 \to 8, \quad \text{Activities: } B \to F \to G}$$

#### Part (b) — Total Float and Free Float Table **[7 Marks]**

For each activity, $ES = E_i$, $EF = ES + t$, $LS = LF - t$, $LF = L_j$, $TF = LS - ES$, $FF = E_j - EF$.

| Activity | i→j | $t$ | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | FF |
|----------|------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| A | 1→2 | 4 | 0 | 4 | 2 | 6 | 2 | 0 |
| B | 1→3 | 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 | 6 | **0** | 0 |
| C | 2→4 | 3 | 4 | 7 | 6 | 9 | 2 | 0 |
| D | 2→5 | 5 | 4 | 9 | 8 | 13 | 4 | 0 |
| E | 6→7 | 4 | 7 | 11 | 9 | 13 | 2 | 2 |
| F | 3→7 | 7 | 6 | 13 | 6 | 13 | **0** | 0 |
| G | 7→8 | 2 | 13 | 15 | 13 | 15 | **0** | 0 |

✅ Critical activities (TF = 0): **B, F, G**. Project Duration = **15 weeks**.

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme]**

**A construction project has the following data. Indirect cost is ₹500 per day. Find the optimum project duration and minimum total cost by crashing.**

| Activity | Normal Time (days) | Normal Cost (₹) | Crash Time (days) | Crash Cost (₹) |
|----------|--------------------|------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1–2 | 6 | 800 | 4 | 1400 |
| 1–3 | 8 | 600 | 6 | 1200 |
| 2–3 | 4 | 500 | 2 | 900 |
| 2–4 | 9 | 1200 | 6 | 1800 |
| 3–4 | 5 | 700 | 3 | 1100 |

#### Part (a) — Cost Slopes and Normal Critical Path **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute Cost Slopes:**

$$CS_{12} = \frac{1400-800}{6-4} = \frac{600}{2} = ₹300/\text{day}$$

$$CS_{13} = \frac{1200-600}{8-6} = \frac{400}{2} = ₹200/\text{day}$$

$$CS_{23} = \frac{900-500}{4-2} = \frac{400}{2} = ₹200/\text{day}$$

$$CS_{24} = \frac{1800-1200}{9-6} = \frac{600}{3} = ₹200/\text{day}$$

$$CS_{34} = \frac{1100-700}{5-3} = \frac{400}{2} = ₹200/\text{day}$$

**Step 2 — Identify Normal Critical Path:**

Possible paths:
- 1–2–3–4 : $6+4+5 = 15$ days
- 1–2–4   : $6+9 = 15$ days ← tied
- 1–3–4   : $8+5 = 13$ days

**Two critical paths** (A–C–E and A–D) at **15 days**.

Normal direct cost = $800+600+500+1200+700 = ₹3800$.
Normal indirect cost = $15 \times 500 = ₹7500$.
**Total normal cost = ₹11,300.**

#### Part (b) — Iterative Crashing for Optimum Cost **[7 Marks]**

**Iteration 1:** Reduce by 1 day; must shorten both 1–2–3–4 and 1–2–4.
- Common to both: 1–2 (₹300).
- Cheaper combo: 1–2(₹300) + any from each path.
- 1–2 alone shortens both: ₹300. **Crash 1–2** (6→5).
- New: 1–2–3–4 = $5+4+5=14$, 1–2–4 = $5+9=14$, 1–3–4 = $8+5=13$. 
- Cost: $₹3800 + 300 + 14 \times 500 = ₹11,100$.

**Iteration 2:** Both paths 1–2–3–4 and 1–2–4 still critical.
- 1–2 common (₹300).
- Cheapest combo: 1–2 + (anything) = 1–2 alone shortens both = ₹300.
- Crash 1–2: $5 \to 4$ (limit). 
- New: 1–2–3–4 = $4+4+5=13$, 1–2–4 = $4+9=13$, 1–3–4 = $8+5=13$.
- **All three critical at 13 days.** Cost: $₹3800+600 + 13 \times 500 = ₹11,100$.

**Iteration 3:** All three critical. Must reduce all simultaneously.
- 1–2 cannot crash (limit = 4).
- Cheapest combo = pick 1 from each path:
  - 1–2–3–4: 1–2(out), 2–3(₹200), 3–4(₹200)
  - 1–2–4: 1–2(out), 2–4(₹200)
  - 1–3–4: 1–3(₹200), 3–4(₹200)
- Best: 2–3 + 2–4 + 1–3 = $200+200+200 = ₹600$ (each path shortened by 1).
- Crash all three: 2–3(4→3), 2–4(9→8), 1–3(8→7).
- New: 1–2–3–4 = $4+3+5=12$, 1–2–4 = $4+8=12$, 1–3–4 = $7+5=12$. 
- Cost: $₹3800+1200 + 12 \times 500 = ₹11,000$.

**Iteration 4:** All three critical at 12. Same combination works.
- 2–3 → 2 (limit). So we must change strategy.
- 1–2–3–4: 3–4(₹200); 1–2–4: 2–4(₹200); 1–3–4: 1–3(₹200). Total = ₹600.
- Crash 3–4, 2–4, 1–3: 3–4(5→4), 2–4(8→7), 1–3(7→6, limit).
- New: 1–2–3–4 = $4+2+4=10$, 1–2–4 = $4+7=11$, 1–3–4 = $6+4=10$.
- Now 1–2–4 is **11 days** (longest), 1–2–3–4 and 1–3–4 are **10**. CP = 1–2–4 only.
- Cost: $₹3800+1800 + 11 \times 500 = ₹11,100$.

**Iteration 5:** CP = 1–2–4 at 11. 1–2(₹300) at limit. Only 2–4(₹200) can be crashed.
- Crash 2–4: $7 \to 6$ (limit). Duration = 10.
- All paths: 1–2–3–4 = $4+2+4=10$, 1–2–4 = $4+6=10$, 1–3–4 = $6+4=10$.
- All critical at 10. Cost: $₹3800+2000 + 10 \times 500 = ₹10,800$.

**Iteration 6:** All critical. 1–2 limit = 4, 1–3 limit = 6, 2–3 limit = 2, 2–4 limit = 6, 3–4 limit = 3.
- 1–2–3–4: 3–4(₹200). 1–2–4: none (1–2 out, 2–4 out).
- Cannot shorten 1–2–4 any further — **crashing ends**.

**Summary Table:**

| Iteration | Duration (days) | Direct Cost (₹) | Indirect (₹) | Total (₹) |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|---------------|-----------|
| Normal | 15 | 3800 | 7500 | 11,300 |
| 1 | 14 | 4100 | 7000 | 11,100 |
| 2 | 13 | 4400 | 6500 | **10,900** |
| 3 | 12 | 5000 | 6000 | 11,000 |
| 4 | 11 | 5600 | 5500 | 11,100 |
| 5 | 10 | 5800 | 5000 | 10,800 |

$$\boxed{\text{Optimum Duration} = 10 \text{ days}, \quad \text{Minimum Total Cost} = ₹10{,}800}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Iteration 2:** The total cost of ₹10,900 at 13 days and ₹10,800 at 10 days shows that the minimum is at **10 days** (the last crashing step that reduced all critical paths simultaneously by 1). However, in some textbook solutions iteration 2 is reported as the optimum. Always tabulate fully to be sure.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes — Avoid These!**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting Dummy Activities:** When an activity has **two or more predecessors** in AOA, you MUST introduce a dummy. Missing this gives a logically wrong network and 4–5 marks lost.
> 
> 2. **Wrong Forward/Backward Pass Direction:** Forward pass uses **MAX** (longest path in), Backward pass uses **MIN** (shortest path out). Reversing these gives garbage values. 
> 
> 3. **Miscounting Free Float:** $FF = E_j - E_i - t_{ij}$ — students often write $E_j - E_i$ alone, forgetting to subtract the activity duration. 
> 
> 4. **Crashing only one path:** When multiple paths are critical, crashing an activity that lies on **only one** of them gives **no benefit**. Always crash a combination that shortens **every** critical path. 
> 
> 5. **Crashing Beyond Limits:** $T_c$ is the absolute minimum. Trying to crash past it earns negative marks. 
> 
> 6. **Ignoring the Cost-Slope-vs-Indirect-Cost Rule:** The optimal stopping point is where the marginal crashing cost equals (or just exceeds) the indirect cost saved per day. Stop earlier or later = loss of 2–3 marks. 
> 
> 7. **Not Labelling the Critical Path in the Network:** Always draw the critical path in a **distinct colour / double-line / bold arrows** in the network diagram. KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for visual presentation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorise for KTU Exam**

### 📌 Core Concepts
- [ ] PERT = **Probabilistic, Time-only**, 3 estimates (Beta distribution).
- [ ] CPM = **Deterministic, Time–Cost**, single estimate, supports crashing.
- [ ] AOA networks use **arrows = activities**, circles = events.
- [ ] **Dummy activities** (dashed, zero duration) handle multiple predecessors logically.

### 📌 Critical Path Rules
- [ ] Forward pass: $E_j = \max(E_i + t_{ij})$, start node $E_1 = 0$.
- [ ] Backward pass: $L_i = \min(L_j - t_{ij})$, end node $L_n = E_n$.
- [ ] **Critical path** = path with **zero total float**; it is the **longest path** in the network.
- [ ] **Project duration** = $E_n$ = $L_n$.

### 📌 Float Hierarchy
- [ ] $TF \ge FF \ge IF$ (always, no exceptions).
- [ ] $TF = L_j - E_i - t_{ij}$
- [ ] $FF = E_j - E_i - t_{ij}$
- [ ] $IF = E_j - L_i - t_{ij}$ (take 0 if negative)

### 📌 PERT Formulas
- [ ] Expected time: $t_e = (t_o + 4t_m + t_p)/6$
- [ ] Standard deviation: $\sigma = (t_p - t_o)/6$
- [ ] Project variance: $\sigma_{proj}^2 = \sum \sigma_{cp}^2$ (sum along critical path only)

### 📌 Crashing Formulas
- [ ] Cost slope: $CS = (C_c - C_n) / (T_n - T_c)$
- [ ] Total cost = Direct cost + Indirect cost
- [ ] Optimal duration = where (crashing cost/day) ≈ (indirect cost saved/day)
- [ ] When multiple CP exist, crash the **cheapest combination** that shortens all.

### 📌 Most-Asked KTU Question Patterns
- [ ] "Construct the network and find the critical path" — 7 marks.
- [ ] "Compute Total Float, Free Float for all activities" — 7 marks.
- [ ] "Crash the project for optimum cost" — 14 marks (full question).
- [ ] "Distinguish PERT vs CPM" — 3 marks (Part A favourite).
- [ ] "Explain the importance of float calculation" — 3 marks.

topics.content:

> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Resource Scheduling, Cost Control and Scheduling: Project Cost Control (PERT/Cost), Resource Scheduling and Resource Levelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Project Cost Control (PERT/Cost)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> **PERT/Cost** is a project management methodology that extends the traditional PERT/CPM network by integrating **cost dimensions** alongside time estimates. It establishes a mathematical relationship between the **project completion time** and the **total project cost**, enabling project managers to determine the **optimum cost-time tradeoff** — i.e., the schedule that minimizes total cost while meeting deadline constraints.

### Conceptual Analogy (The "Delivery Truck" Intuition)

Imagine you need to transport 1000 boxes from Kochi to Bengaluru.

- **Normal speed** (cheap fuel, normal truck): Takes 3 days, costs ₹10,000.
- **Express speed** (premium fuel, faster truck): Takes 1 day, costs ₹25,000.
- **You** are the project manager, the **boxes** are project activities, the **days** are time, and the **fuel cost** is the project cost.

The question is: *"What is the cheapest combination of trucks and speeds that gets the boxes there on time?"* — This is precisely what **PERT/Cost** solves. You evaluate trade-offs: spending a little more on a critical activity to save days (and thus **indirect costs** like overheads, rent, salaries).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **Direct Cost (Crashing Cost)**: Money spent *directly* on activities (labor, materials, overtime pay).
> - **Indirect Cost (Overhead)**: Money spent on the *project as a whole* over time (office rent, admin salaries, utilities). Indirect costs **grow linearly with time**.
> - **Total Project Cost** = Direct Cost + Indirect Cost.
> The **optimum project duration** is the point where the **Total Cost Curve** is minimum.

---

## 1.2 Resource Scheduling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Resource Scheduling** is the process of developing a project schedule (activity start and finish times) that takes into account both the **logical dependencies** of the network (from PERT/CPM) and the **availability constraints of resources** (human, equipment, material) required to perform the activities.

### The "Conference Room" Intuition

Suppose your project has 10 activities, but only **1 conference room** (a shared, limited resource). Even if PERT/CPM says two activities can happen on Day 3 simultaneously, you *cannot* — they must be **serialized** because they fight over the same room. **Resource Scheduling** finds the best way to allocate that one room across activities without breaking logical dependencies.

---

## 1.3 Resource Levelling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Resource Levelling** is a resource optimization technique that **smooths the peaks and valleys** of resource demand over the project duration, so that the resource requirement is distributed as evenly as possible. It may extend the project duration, but it produces a **stable, humanely workable resource profile**.

### The "Personal Trainer at a Gym" Intuition

A gym has 1 trainer. On Monday, 50 people want a session (peak). On Wednesday, only 2 people (valley). The trainer is exhausted Monday, idle Wednesday. **Resource Levelling** rebalances the schedule — shifting some Monday appointments to Tuesday/Wednesday — so the trainer handles ~25 clients/day (a **flat, stable workload**). The gym may have to *extend* total operating days, but operations are sustainable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Resource Smoothing vs Resource Levelling — A KTU Favorite!**
> - **Resource Smoothing**: Adjusts activity start times *within the available float* so that resource peaks are reduced, but the **project completion date is NOT changed**. Resource demand never exceeds the **original peak**.
> - **Resource Levelling**: Adjusts activity start times to produce a level resource profile. It may **delay the project completion date** when the resource demand exceeds the available supply.

---

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Memorize for KTU)

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Crash Cost | $C_c$ | ₹ (Currency) | Cost of completing an activity in its minimum (crash) time |
| Normal Cost | $C_n$ | ₹ (Currency) | Cost of completing an activity in its normal time |
| Normal Time | $T_n$ | Days/Weeks | Standard expected duration |
| Crash Time | $T_c$ | Days/Weeks | Absolute minimum achievable duration |
| Cost Slope | $S$ | ₹/Day | **Cost per unit time saved** |
| Indirect Cost | $I$ | ₹/Day | Daily overhead cost |
| Project Duration | $T$ | Days | Total project time |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PERT/Cost: The Cost-Time Relationship

Every activity in a project has a **time-cost curve**. While theoretically curvilinear (convex), in PERT/Cost analysis it is **approximated as a straight line** between two points:
- **Normal Point (Np)**: $(T_n, C_n)$ — Best quality, lowest direct cost, longest time.
- **Crash Point (Cp)**: $(T_c, C_c)$ — Shortest time, highest direct cost.

### Why Linear Approximation?

The **linear approximation** is used because:
1. It dramatically simplifies the mathematical optimization.
2. It provides conservative estimates (convex curve gives a *lower* crash cost than the linear projection).
3. Real-world data is rarely precise enough to justify a curve.

### The Core Crashing Logic — Step by Step

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Crashing:**
> Indirect costs (rent, salaries, administration) accumulate **every day the project runs**. So if you can spend ₹5,000 extra on direct costs to **finish 1 day earlier** and save ₹10,000 in indirect costs, you make a **net saving of ₹5,000**. The process of shortening the schedule by paying more is called **crashing**.

**Step 1 — Compute Cost Slope for each activity:**

$$S = \frac{C_c - C_n}{T_n - T_c}$$

- The **Cost Slope** represents the cost (₹) of saving *one day* on that activity.
- A **lower slope** = cheaper to crash.

**Step 2 — Identify the Critical Path** using CPM (forward & backward pass).

**Step 3 — Crash the cheapest critical activity** (lowest cost slope on the critical path), provided it has not already reached its crash time.

**Step 4 — Re-evaluate.** When you crash, the critical path may shift. Always recompute the network.

**Step 5 — Stop** when:
- The desired project duration is reached, OR
- All critical path activities are fully crashed.

---

## 2.2 The Total Project Cost Curve

$$\text{Total Cost}(T) = \text{Direct Cost}(T) + \text{Indirect Cost}(T)$$

| Component | Behavior as T increases | Behavior as T decreases |
|---|---|---|
| Direct Cost | Decreases (less crashing needed) | Increases (more crashing) |
| Indirect Cost | Increases (longer project) | Decreases (shorter project) |
| **Total Cost** | **U-shaped curve (has a minimum)** | U-shaped curve (has a minimum) |

The **optimum project duration (T_opt)** is the point where the **Total Cost is minimum**. At this point, the slope of the total cost curve is zero.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:**
> At the optimum point, the cost slope of crashing equals the indirect cost per day:
>
> $$\text{Cost Slope of cheapest critical activity} = \text{Indirect Cost per day}$$
>
> If slope < indirect cost → **Crash more** (you save money).
> If slope > indirect cost → **Stop crashing** (you lose money).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Rule | LaTeX Expression | Purpose / When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cost Slope | $S = \frac{C_c - C_n}{T_n - T_c}$ | Determines crash cost per day for any activity |
| 2 | Direct Cost after k days of crashing | $C_{direct} = C_{normal} + k \cdot S$ | Cumulative direct cost of an activity |
| 3 | Total Project Cost | $C_{total} = C_{direct} + (I \cdot T)$ | Used to find optimum duration |
| 4 | Optimum Condition | $S_{critical} = I$ | Stopping criterion for crashing |
| 5 | Max crash days per activity | $k_{max} = T_n - T_c$ | Upper bound on how much an activity can be crashed |
| 6 | Project float (slack) | $SL = LF - EF$ | Identifies non-critical activities (candidates for resource levelling) |
| 7 | Resource profile peak | $\text{Peak} = \max_{t} R(t)$ | Used to compare against resource limit |
| 8 | Average resource demand | $\bar{R} = \frac{\sum_{i} r_i \cdot d_i}{T}$ | Target for levelled resource profile |
| 9 | Resource utilisation factor | $U = \frac{\bar{R}}{\text{Peak}}$ | Should approach 1.0 for good levelling |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Software Industry** | Determining whether to add more developers (crash cost) vs. extending the release date (indirect cost of delayed market entry). |
| **Construction** | Crashing: Paying overtime vs. delaying handover (penalty vs. overhead). |
| **Aerospace/Defense** | Allocating limited expert engineers across parallel work packages. |
| **IT Service Delivery** | Levelling server loads across 24 hours to avoid peak-hour crashes. |
| **Manufacturing** | Distributing assembly line workload to prevent worker burnout. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Comprehensive Numerical Example: Project Crashing (PERT/Cost)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Style Problem:** The following is a **classic 14-mark exam question pattern**. Master this exact format.

### Problem Statement

A small software project has **5 activities (A, B, C, D, E)** with the following data:

| Activity | Predecessor | Normal Time (days) | Normal Cost (₹) | Crash Time (days) | Crash Cost (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | — | 6 | 1000 | 4 | 2000 |
| B | A | 8 | 1500 | 6 | 2500 |
| C | A | 4 | 800 | 2 | 1600 |
| D | B | 5 | 1200 | 3 | 1800 |
| E | C, D | 7 | 2000 | 5 | 3000 |

**Indirect cost** = ₹200 per day. Find the **optimum project duration** and **minimum total cost**.

---

### Step 1 — Compute Cost Slope for Each Activity

Using the formula $S = \frac{C_c - C_n}{T_n - T_c}$:

**Activity A:**

$$S_A = \frac{2000 - 1000}{6 - 4} = \frac{1000}{2} = ₹500/\text{day}$$

**Activity B:**

$$S_B = \frac{2500 - 1500}{8 - 6} = \frac{1000}{2} = ₹500/\text{day}$$

**Activity C:**

$$S_C = \frac{1600 - 800}{4 - 2} = \frac{800}{2} = ₹400/\text{day}$$

**Activity D:**

$$S_D = \frac{1800 - 1200}{5 - 3} = \frac{600}{2} = ₹300/\text{day}$$

**Activity E:**

$$S_E = \frac{3000 - 2000}{7 - 5} = \frac{1000}{2} = ₹500/\text{day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Summary Table of Cost Slopes:**
>
> | Activity | Cost Slope (₹/day) |
> |---|---|
> | A | 500 |
> | B | 500 |
> | C | 400 |
> | D | **300** ← Cheapest |
> | E | 500 |

---

### Step 2 — Initial CPM Analysis (Normal Times)

Network paths:
- **Path 1:** A → B → D → E = 6 + 8 + 5 + 7 = **26 days**
- **Path 2:** A → C → E = 6 + 4 + 7 = **17 days**

**Critical Path** = Path 1 (A-B-D-E), Duration = **26 days**.

---

### Step 3 — Normal Project Cost Calculation
