## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the Agile Manifesto. List its four core values.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Agile Manifesto** (2001) is a foundational document authored by 17 software practitioners that articulates a value-driven, iterative approach to software development. Its four core values are:

1. **Individuals and interactions** over processes and tools
2. **Working software** over comprehensive documentation
3. **Customer collaboration** over contract negotiation
4. **Responding to change** over following a plan

> [!NOTE]
> *Valuation Tip:* Students often lose 1 mark by omitting that the right-hand items still have value — the left-hand items are *valued more*, not exclusive.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a Sprint in Scrum? Mention its typical duration.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Sprint** is the basic unit of time in the Scrum framework during which a usable, potentially shippable product increment is created. It is a fixed time-box, typically **1 to 4 weeks** (most commonly **2 weeks**). The Sprint is bounded by Sprint Planning and concludes with a Sprint Review and Retrospective. Once started, the duration remains constant to allow consistent cadence and learning.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Option 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain in detail the 12 principles of the Agile Manifesto with relevant examples for a software project.*

**Model Answer:**

The 12 Agile Principles, as defined in the Agile Manifesto, are:

1. **Customer satisfaction through early and continuous delivery** — Deliver software in 2-week sprints to give the customer working features fast. *Example:* Releasing a working login module in Sprint 1.

2. **Welcome changing requirements, even late in development** — A new compliance requirement is incorporated in the next sprint instead of being rejected.

3. **Deliver working software frequently** — Biweekly releases instead of a single annual release.

4. **Business and developers must work together daily** — Product Owner sits with the dev team and resolves queries on the same day.

5. **Build projects around motivated individuals** — Give the team autonomy in technical decisions; no micromanagement of coding style.

6. **Face-to-face conversation** — Daily 15-minute stand-ups and pair programming for complex modules.

7. **Working software is the primary measure of progress** — Demo at Sprint Review; not "80% code written."

8. **Sustainable development pace** — 40-hour work weeks, no "death marches."

9. **Continuous attention to technical excellence** — Refactoring, TDD, peer code reviews in every sprint.

10. **Simplicity — the art of maximizing the amount of work not done** — Apply YAGNI; don't build features "just in case."

11. **Self-organizing teams** — Team decides *how* to deliver, not the manager.

12. **Regular reflection and adjustment** — Sprint Retrospective identifies process improvements (e.g., better testing strategy).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** These principles collectively enable faster feedback, higher quality, and customer-aligned delivery in dynamic software projects.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Compare Agile and Waterfall project management approaches across 7 key parameters. When would you prefer Agile over Waterfall?*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Waterfall | Agile |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Approach** | Sequential, linear | Iterative, incremental |
| **2. Requirements** | Fixed at the start | Evolving, emergent |
| **3. Delivery** | Single delivery at project end | Frequent, incremental releases |
| **4. Customer Involvement** | At phase boundaries | Continuous throughout |
| **5. Documentation** | Extensive, formal | Minimal, just-in-time |
| **6. Risk Identification** | Late in lifecycle | Early and continuous |
| **7. Team Structure** | Specialized silos | Cross-functional |

**When to Prefer Agile (1 mark for concluding remark):**

Agile is preferred when:
- Requirements are **uncertain or evolving** (e.g., new product, startup).
- The project requires **rapid time-to-market**.
- Customer feedback is **critical** and frequent.
- The team is **co-located or well-coordinated**.
- Technology stack is **emerging** (AI, cloud-native).

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Agile is best suited for complex, dynamic projects; Waterfall is suitable for well-understood, regulatory, or hardware-software embedded projects with fixed requirements.

---

#### **Question B (Option 2) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the Scrum framework in detail. List the 3 roles, 5 events, and 3 artifacts with their responsibilities.*

**Model Answer:**

**Scrum** is an agile framework for developing, delivering, and sustaining complex products, created by **Ken Schwaber and Jeff Sutherland**.

**3 Roles (2 marks):**

1. **Product Owner (PO):** Owns the Product Backlog, prioritizes features based on business value, and is the single voice of the customer.
2. **Scrum Master (SM):** Serves the team by removing impediments, facilitating Scrum events, and coaching the organization on Scrum.
3. **Development Team:** Self-organizing, cross-functional team of 3–9 members who deliver the increment.

**5 Events (2 marks):**

1. **Sprint** — 1–4 week time-box for creating an increment.
2. **Sprint Planning** — Team selects Product Backlog items for the Sprint.
3. **Daily Scrum** — 15-minute daily sync meeting.
4. **Sprint Review** — Demo to stakeholders, gather feedback.
5. **Sprint Retrospective** — Team inspects and improves its process.

**3 Artifacts (2 marks):**

1. **Product Backlog** — Ordered list of all desired features.
2. **Sprint Backlog** — Set of items + plan for the current Sprint.
3. **Increment** — Sum of completed items, meeting the Definition of Done.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Scrum's lightweight structure enables empirical process control through transparency, inspection, and adaptation.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Discuss the relationship between Agile, Lean, DevOps, and ITIL. Explain how they integrate to deliver business value.*

**Model Answer:**

These four frameworks are **complementary**, each addressing a different stage or dimension of software delivery and operations.

**1. Agile and Lean (2 marks):**
Lean provides the **philosophy** (eliminate waste, optimize flow, deliver fast, build integrity in). Agile operationalizes Lean through **iterative delivery, short cycles, and customer collaboration**. The Agile Manifesto draws heavily on Lean principles. Lean informs *what* to build; Agile determines *how* to build it incrementally.

**2. Agile and DevOps (2 marks):**
Agile focuses on *building* the right product. DevOps focuses on *deploying and operating* it reliably. Together, they form the **Continuous Delivery pipeline**: Agile produces the increment → DevOps automates its build, test, and release. DevOps extends agility to operations through CI/CD, IaC, and monitoring.

**3. Agile and ITIL (1.5 marks):**
ITIL v4 (2019) explicitly embraces agile values. ITIL's **7 guiding principles** (focus on value, start where you are, progress iteratively, collaborate, think holistically, keep it simple, optimize) overlap significantly with Agile. While Agile governs *product development*, ITIL governs *service management* (incident, problem, change, and request management). They integrate via ITIL's Service Value Chain (SVC) which incorporates agile workflows.

**4. Integrated Chain (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{Lean (Strategy)} \rightarrow \text{Agile/Scrum (Build)} \rightarrow \text{DevOps (Deploy)} \rightarrow \text{ITIL (Operate)} \rightarrow \text{Business Value}
$$

**Conclusion (Bonus):** Modern enterprises adopt all four — Lean for efficiency, Agile for product development, DevOps for delivery automation, and ITIL for service governance — to achieve end-to-end value delivery.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Agile Questions:**
> 1. **Listing the four values but missing the right-hand items** — Always say "X over Y" (not just "X").
> 2. **Confusing Agile with Scrum** — Agile is a *mindset*; Scrum is a *framework* (a subset of Agile).
> 3. **Mixing up DevOps roles with Scrum roles** — DevOps has no Scrum Master; it has SREs, Release Engineers.
> 4. **Forgetting Sprint duration constraints** — Daily Scrum is **strictly 15 minutes**; this is a common question.
> 5. **Not mentioning Definition of Done** — Required for Increment to be considered complete.
> 6. **In ITIL questions, missing the version** — Always specify **ITIL v4 (2019)** in your answer.
> 7. **In calculations, missing units** — Velocity is in **story points/sprint**, not just story points.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Rapid Revision Bullets:**
> - ✅ Agile is a **mindset**; Scrum is a **framework**; Lean is a **philosophy**; DevOps is a **culture**; ITIL is a **service management framework**.
> - ✅ Agile Manifesto has **4 values** and **12 principles**.
> - ✅ Scrum has **3 roles** (PO, SM, Dev Team), **5 events**, **3 artifacts**.
> - ✅ Sprint is **1–4 weeks**; Daily Scrum is **15 minutes**; Sprint is **fixed duration**.
> - ✅ Velocity = average story points/sprint; used for **release forecasting**.
> - ✅ Burndown chart tracks **remaining work over time**.
> - ✅ **Definition of Done** is required for an increment to be considered complete.
> - ✅ Lean emphasizes **eliminating waste, building integrity, delivering fast**.
> - ✅ DevOps = **CI + CD + IaC + Monitoring** — bridges Dev and Ops.
> - ✅ **ITIL v4** has 7 guiding principles and 34 management practices.
> - ✅ Agile + DevOps = Continuous Delivery; Agile + ITIL = Service-oriented agility.
> - ✅ The integrated chain: **Lean → Agile → DevOps → ITIL → Business Value**.
> - ✅ Scrum Master is a **servant-leader**, not a project manager.
> - ✅ Self-organizing teams choose *how* to do the work.
> - ✅ In KTU, mention **authors and years** (e.g., Schwaber & Sutherland, 1995) wherever possible — it earns ½ to 1 mark.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to "Other Agile Methodologies"

While **Scrum** dominates the industry headlines, the Agile Manifesto (2001) inspired a rich **constellation of alternative Agile methodologies** beyond Scrum. The KTU 2024 syllabus groups four of the most academically significant ones under the umbrella of "Other Agile Methodologies" — **Extreme Programming (XP)**, **Feature Driven Development (FDD)**, **Dynamic Systems Development Method (DSDM)**, and the **Crystal family**. Each methodology offers a distinct philosophical and procedural lens for handling iterative, incremental software delivery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** These methodologies are typically covered as **Part B, 14-mark descriptive questions**. Students are expected to compare their core values, lifecycle phases, and distinctive engineering practices. Recall of founders and key principles is heavily rewarded in valuation.

---

## 1.2 Extreme Programming (XP)

**Definition:** *Extreme Programming (XP)* is a lightweight, code-centric Agile software development methodology that advocates a set of **engineering practices designed to improve software quality and responsiveness to changing customer requirements**. It was conceived by **Kent Beck** in the late 1990s during the Chrysler C3 payroll project.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (PECST521):** Extreme Programming is a discipline of software development based on values of simplicity, communication, feedback, and courage. It involves close collaboration with customers, frequent releases in short iterations, and a strong emphasis on testing and refactoring.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Surgical Team" of Chefs

> [!NOTE]
> Think of XP as a **high-pressure Michelin-star kitchen**. The **Customer (or Chef Patron)** walks in daily, tastes the dish, and changes the menu. The **Developers (Sous Chefs)** are paired up — one cooks, the other watches, then they switch. Every dish (release) is tasted (tested) before leaving the kitchen. The recipes are kept simple, and every plate is checked, not trusted blindly. This is XP — **engineering discipline taken to the extreme**.

### 1.2.2 The Four Core Values of XP

| # | Value | Meaning (in simple terms) |
|---|-------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Communication** | Everyone talks openly — no hidden requirements. |
| 2 | **Simplicity** | Do what is needed today; don't over-engineer. |
| 3 | **Feedback** | Test early, demo often, learn continuously. |
| 4 | **Courage** | Tell the truth about progress; refactor without fear. |

---

## 1.3 Feature Driven Development (FDD)

**Definition:** *Feature Driven Development (FDD)* is a model-driven, **short-iteration Agile methodology** that focuses on delivering tangible, client-valued **features** rather than abstract tasks. It was created by **Jeff De Luca** and **Peter Coad** in **1997** at a large Singapore banking project.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** FDD is an iterative and incremental software development process that organizes system functionality into a list of features and then performs design and build activities on a feature-by-feature basis. It blends the best practices of agile methods with the rigor of model-driven engineering.

### 1.3.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Menu"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine running a **diner where every item on the menu must be perfect, one dish at a time**. The chef doesn't say "I'll cook a 'meal'". Instead, he says "I'll cook a *Masala Dosa with Coconut Chutney* — a clearly defined feature." Each dish (feature) is small enough to cook in days, valuable to the customer, and walk-able-through. FDD structures work as a **menu of features**, not a blob of requirements.

### 1.3.2 The Five FDD Processes (Lifecycle Macro-Process)

The FDD process is structured into **5 sequential activities** (the famous "**Coad's 5**"):

1. **Develop an Overall Model**
2. **Build a Features List**
3. **Plan by Feature**
4. **Design by Feature**
5. **Build by Feature**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> FDD is widely considered the **most scalable Agile method** because the feature list is built once and consumed repeatedly. The official KTU textbook "Software Project Management by Bob Hughes et al." gives this method 14-mark weightage.

---

## 1.4 Dynamic Systems Development Method (DSDM)

**Definition:** *Dynamic Systems Development Method (DSDM)* is a framework for **rapid application development (RAD)** that provides a comprehensive foundation for **planning, managing, controlling, and executing software projects**. Originally developed in the **United Kingdom in 1994** by a consortium of vendors, DSDM was later revised into **DSDM Atern** in 2007.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** DSDM is an Agile framework that addresses the common failings of traditional software projects by emphasizing active user involvement, empowered teams, frequent delivery, and the use of fitness-for-purpose timeboxes.

### 1.4.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Construction Site with a Hard Deadline"

> [!NOTE]
> DSDM operates like a **government building project with a fixed handover date**. The deadline (the **timebox**) cannot move. To cope with this constraint, the builders use a **prioritized backlog** (MoSCoW) — only **Must-haves** get built; **Won't-haves** are cut. The customer is on-site daily. The skeleton is erected first (the **iterative** part), then walls, then finishes — each in fixed windows.

### 1.4.2 DSDM Atern — The Modern Version

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Post-2007, DSDM was rebranded as **DSDM Atern** (the word "Atern" is a deliberate spelling of *iterate*). The Atern version simplifies the framework into **3 phases**: **Foundations → Exploration → Engineering → Deployment** (actually 4 phases), wrapped in a **Feasibility Phase**.

---

## 1.5 The Crystal Family of Methodologies

**Definition:** *Crystal* is a **family of methodologies** developed by **Alistair Cockburn** in the early 1990s. Its central thesis is that **the optimal methodology depends on project size, criticality, and team priority**, so Cockburn crafted multiple color-coded variants — **Crystal Clear (small teams, 1–6 people)**, **Crystal Yellow (8–20 people)**, **Crystal Orange (20–40)**, and so on, up to **Crystal Red/Violet** for mission-critical, large-scale systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Crystal is a family of Agile methodologies characterized by their human-centric, adaptability-driven design. The colors represent increasing project criticality and team size, each tuned to the "comfort zone" of the team.

### 1.5.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wardrobe of Suits"

> [!NOTE]
> Crystal is like a **tailor offering different suits for different occasions**. You don't wear a tuxedo to play football, nor shorts to a board meeting. Cockburn says: **the project should wear the methodology that fits it**. A 3-person startup uses **Crystal Clear** (lightweight); a 30-person banking system uses **Crystal Orange** (more discipline). The "suit" can change as the project grows.

### 1.5.2 The Seven Core Properties of Crystal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Cockburn identified **seven core properties** that any successful project must have, regardless of color. The heavier the project (by criticality + team size), the more rigorously these properties are enforced.

| # | Property | One-Line Meaning |
|---|----------|------------------|
| 1 | Frequent Delivery | Ship working software often. |
| 2 | Reflective Improvement | Tune the process at the end of each iteration. |
| 3 | Close Communication | Co-location / Osmotic communication. |
| 4 | Personal Safety | Speak up without fear. |
| 5 | Focus | Everyone knows the priority. |
| 6 | Easy Access to Expert Users | Subject-matter experts are on tap. |
| 7 | Technical Environment | Automated tests, config management, fast feedback. |

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 XP — Engineering Practices (The Heart of XP)

XP is more famous for its **12 engineering practices** than for any process flow. These are usually **grouped into 4 quadrants** in KTU questions.

### 2.1.1 The 12 XP Practices (Grouped)

**Quadrant 1 — Fine-Scale Feedback**
- **Pair Programming** — two developers, one screen, one keyboard.
- **Planning Game** — joint customer + developer planning in each iteration.
- **Test-Driven Development (TDD)** — write the test *before* the code.
- **Whole Team** — all skills needed are on the team, including customer representatives.

**Quadrant 2 — Continuous Process**
- **Continuous Integration** — integrate and test every few hours.
- **Design Improvement (Refactoring)** — improve internal structure without changing behavior.
- **Small Releases** — release the smallest useful system often.

**Quadrant 3 — Shared Understanding**
- **Coding Standards** — every team member writes code that looks like one person's.
- **Collective Code Ownership** — anyone can fix any code.
- **Simple Design** — do only what is needed, no more.
- **System Metaphor** — a simple shared story of how the system works.

**Quadrant 4 — Programmer Welfare**
- **Sustainable Pace** — teams work at a constant, healthy velocity (~40-hour weeks).

### 2.1.2 XP Roles (KTU Favourite 3-Mark Question)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises **role clarity** in XP. The 6 primary roles are: **Programmer, Customer, Coach, Tracker, Consultant, Big Boss (Manager)**. For 3-mark questions, memorise that **Customer = on-site user representative who writes user stories**, and **Coach = process enforcer**.

---

## 2.2 FDD — Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 FDD's Best Practices (Original 8)

| # | Best Practice | Meaning |
|---|---------------|---------|
| 1 | Domain Object Modeling | Build class diagrams per feature area. |
| 2 | Developing by Feature | Small client-valued chunks. |
| 3 | Individual Class (Code) Ownership | Each class owned by one developer. |
| 4 | Feature Teams | Dynamically formed per feature. |
| 5 | Inspections | Code reviews, walkthroughs. |
| 6 | Regular Builds | Daily or more frequent. |
| 7 | Configuration Management | Track all artifacts. |
| 8 | Visibility of Progress & Results | Cumulative flow visible to all. |

### 2.2.2 FDD — The Concept of a "Feature"

> [!NOTE]
> A **Feature** in FDD is expressed in the form: *"<action> <object> <result> <by/for> <stakeholder>"* — for example, **"Calculate the total of the user's basket"**. Features are categorized into **subject areas** (logical groups) and assigned a priority. The KTU examiner often asks: *Why is a feature a better unit of work than a use case?* — because features are **client-valued, small, and estimable in days**.

### 2.2.3 FDD Lifecycle Equations & Metrics

$$
\text{Total Features} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i
$$

$$
\text{Project Velocity} = \frac{\text{Features Completed}}{\text{Working Days Elapsed}}
$$

$$
\text{Progress (\%)} = \frac{\text{Features Built}}{\text{Features Planned}} \times 100
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> FDD maintains a **progress chart** updated after every feature. Unlike Scrum, FDD tracks **exact feature completion %**, not story points.

---

## 2.3 DSDM — The 9 Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> DSDM's 9 principles are the most-tested DSDM concept in KTU exams. Memorise all 9.

1. **Active user involvement is imperative.**
2. **DSDM teams must be empowered to make decisions.**
3. **The focus is on frequent delivery of products.**
4. **Fitness for business purpose is the essential criterion for acceptance of deliverables.**
5. **Iterative and incremental development is necessary to converge on an accurate business solution.**
6. **All changes during development are reversible.**
7. **Requirements are baselined at a high level.**
8. **Testing is integrated throughout the lifecycle.**
9. **A collaborative and co-operative approach between all stakeholders is essential.**

### 2.3.1 The MoSCoW Prioritization

> [!NOTE]
> **MoSCoW** is DSDM's flagship prioritization technique. KTU frequently asks for **the 4 categories** and one **example each**.

| Letter | Category | Description | Typical Effort |
|--------|----------|-------------|----------------|
| **M** | Must have | Critical for release; if missing, the release fails. | 60% |
| **S** | Should have | Important, but release can survive without. | 20% |
| **C** | Could have | Nice to have, only if time permits. | 20% |
| **W** | Won't have (this time) | Deferred to a future release. | 0% (this iteration) |

### 2.3.2 DSDM Timeboxing

$$
T_{\text{box}} = T_{\text{develop}} + T_{\text{review}} + T_{\text{consolidate}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **timebox is fixed**. Scope is variable. If a Must-have cannot be completed inside the timebox, the team **de-scopes** the *Won't-haves* or *Could-haves* first — never the Must-haves.

### 2.3.3 DSDM Atern Phases

| Phase | Purpose | Key Output |
|-------|---------|------------|
| **Feasibility** | Can we do it? | Feasibility report. |
| **Foundations** | Agree on the big picture. | Initial requirements, MoSCoW list. |
| **Exploration** | Iteratively refine and prototype. | Refined requirements, prototype. |
| **Engineering** | Build the production solution. | Iterative increments. |
| **Deployment** | Release to users. | Live system. |

---

## 2.4 Crystal — Color-Coded Variants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Color = Team Size + Criticality** is a routine KTU question.

| Color | Team Size | Criticality | Rigour Level |
|-------|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| **Crystal Clear** | 1–6 | Low (C, D) | Lightweight |
| **Crystal Yellow** | 6–20 | Low (C, D) | Light–Medium |
| **Crystal Orange** | 20–40 | Medium (E) | Medium |
| **Crystal Red** | 40–80 | High (F) | Heavy |
| **Crystal Maroon** | 80+ | Mission-critical (G) | Heaviest |

### 2.4.1 Cockburn's Formula for Methodology Choice

Cockburn observed that the **discipline burden** of a methodology should grow with team size and criticality:

$$
D = f(\text{Team Size},\ \text{Criticality})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> As one goes from **Crystal Clear → Maroon**, more artefacts, reviews, and standards are required. **Crystal Clear** may need only sticky notes and daily stand-ups; **Crystal Red/Maroon** may require formal configurations, sign-offs, and metrics.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Lifecycle Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 XP — Full Lifecycle (Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often ask: *Explain the XP project lifecycle in detail with the help of a flowchart (14 marks).* Memorize the 6 phases below with the deliverable of each.

### 3.1.1 The 6 XP Phases — Derivation / Walkthrough

$$
\text{XP Lifecycle} = \text{Exploration} \to \text{Planning} \to \text{Iterations-to-First-Release} \to \text{Productionizing} \to \text{Maintenance} \to \text{Death}
$$

**Step 1 — Exploration (1–2 weeks).**  
The customer writes **user stories** (small, card-sized requirements). The team explores the tools and technologies. Output: a set of user stories on index cards.

**Step 2 — Planning (1–2 days per release cycle).**  
Customers prioritize stories; developers estimate effort. Stories are committed into releases of 1–2 months.

**Step 3 — Iterations to First Release (1–4 weeks each).**  
The team picks stories, implements them in **1–2 week iterations**, using **pair programming**, **TDD**, and **continuous integration**. Output: a small working release.

**Step 4 — Productionizing (additional iteration).**  
Performance, deployment, and user-acceptance tests are added. A **new release** is shipped to live users.

**Step 5 — Maintenance.**  
New user stories are continuously added; the team operates the system while developing enhancements.

**Step 6 — Death.**  
When the customer has no more stories, the system is documented, lessons are recorded, and the project is closed.

### 3.1.2 XP — Velocity Calculation (Worked Numerical)

**Example (KTU-style):**  
A team completes **8 user stories** worth an average of **3 points** each in a **2-week iteration**. The team's previous velocity was **20 points/iteration**. Determine the team's progress ratio.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{current}} &= \text{Stories Completed} \times \text{Average Points} \\
&= 8 \times 3 \\
&= 24 \text{ points} \\[6pt]
\text{Progress Ratio} &= \frac{V_{\text{current}}}{V_{\text{baseline}}} = \frac{24}{20} = 1.20
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A progress ratio of **1.20** means the team is **20 % more productive** than its baseline. A ratio below 0.80 triggers a velocity review meeting in XP. **Always quote the unit ("points/iteration") in your KTU answer.**

---

## 3.2 FDD — Step-by-Step Walkthrough with a Real Example

> [!NOTE]
> Below is a **derivation-style walkthrough** of building an online e-commerce system using FDD. The KTU 2024 paper Dec 2023 had a near-identical problem.

### 3.2.1 FDD Step 1 — Develop an Overall Model

**Input:** Domain experts and developers collaborate to build a **high-level class/object model** of the problem domain.  
**Output:** An **object model** with class diagrams.

### 3.2.2 FDD Step 2 — Build a Features List

Features are derived from the model using the template:

$$
F = \langle \text{action, object, result, by, stakeholder} \rangle
$$

**Example features for e-commerce:**

| # | Feature (F) |
|---|-------------|
| 1 | *Add a product to the shopping cart by a registered user* |
| 2 | *Calculate the total price of the cart for the customer* |
| 3 | *Display the order history to a registered user* |
| 4 | *Generate an invoice for a placed order* |

The features are grouped into **subject areas** (e.g., *Cart Management*, *Order Management*, *User Management*).

### 3.2.3 FDD Step 3 — Plan by Feature

The Chief Programmer assigns each feature to a **Class Owner**. The next 2–3 weeks of work are scheduled in dependency order.  
**Output:** A **development plan** and **class-owner assignments**.

### 3.2.4 FDD Step 4 — Design by Feature

For each feature, the **Class Owner** produces a **design package**:
- Sequence diagrams
- Detailed class diagrams
- Method signatures
- Test specifications

**Output:** A **Feature Design Document**.

### 3.2.5 FDD Step 5 — Build by Feature

The class owner (and possibly a feature team) **implements, unit-tests, code-reviews, and integrates** the feature into the main build. A feature is officially *Done* only when it has been built, inspected, and is part of the main build.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A feature is **not considered complete** in FDD until it is **inspected**. The KTU examiner always stresses this — *inspec­tion is part of "Build by Feature"*.

### 3.2.6 FDD Progress Calculation

**Example:** A project has **120 planned features**. After 30 working days, the team has *Built* **45 features**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\%) &= \frac{F_{\text{built}}}{F_{\text{planned}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{45}{120} \times 100 \\
&= 37.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The progress chart is updated, and the **cumulative flow graph** is published.

---

## 3.3 DSDM — Worked Scenario

**Scenario (KTU type):** A 5-month DSDM project is in the Engineering Phase. The team has 200 requirements. The current timebox is 4 weeks. MoSCoW allocation: M = 100, S = 50, C = 50, W = 0. By week 2, only 40 Must-haves are done.

### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Velocity Check

$$
V_{\text{M}} = \frac{40 \text{ must-haves}}{2 \text{ weeks}} = 20 \text{ must-haves/week}
$$

### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Forecast Completion

$$
T_{\text{remaining}} = \frac{100 - 40}{20} = 3 \text{ weeks}
$$

### 3.3.3 Step 3 — De-scope Decision

Since the timebox is 2 more weeks, the team will **not** finish all 100 Must-haves. DSDM protocol:

- **Timebox is sacred** — it cannot be extended.
- The team **de-scopes the Could-haves first** (50 features). 
- If still insufficient, Should-haves are deferred.

$$
\text{Scope Reduction} = \min\!\big(\text{Would-be overrun features},\ \text{available slack}\big)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In DSDM, **Must-haves are protected**; we de-scope downward in the MoSCoW hierarchy. This is a top-of-the-list valuation point.

---

## 3.4 Crystal — Decision Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus often frames a question: *"Given a team of 12 people building a medical system with life-critical consequences, which Crystal variant fits and why?"*

### 3.4.1 Step-by-Step Decision Logic

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If team size} &\le 6 \text{ AND criticality } \in \{C,D\} : \text{Use Crystal Clear} \\
\text{Else if team size} &\in [6,20] \text{ AND criticality } \in \{C,D\} : \text{Use Crystal Yellow} \\
\text{Else if team size} &\in [20,40] \text{ AND criticality } \in \{D,E\} : \text{Use Crystal Orange} \\
\text{Else if team size} &\in [40,80] \text{ AND criticality } \in \{E,F\} : \text{Use Crystal Red} \\
\text{Else :} &\ \text{Use Crystal Maroon / Crystal Diamond}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Criticality letters** come from Cockburn's "Losses from Failure" scale:  
> **C** = Comfort loss, **D** = Discretionary money loss, **E** = Essential money loss, **F** = Life-critical loss, **G** = Loss of many lives.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU 3-Mark Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **single most important comparison** for KTU Part A 3-mark questions. Memorize it.

| Dimension | XP | FDD | DSDM | Crystal |
|-----------|----|----|------|---------|
| **Founder** | Kent Beck | De Luca & Coad | DSDM Consortium | Alistair Cockburn |
| **Core unit** | User Story | Feature | Timeboxed increment | Iteration |
| **Customer role** | On-site, full-time | Domain expert walk-through | Active user | Easy access to expert users |

| **Best for** | Small, changing requirements | Large, long-term projects | Fixed deadlines, RAD | Variable, human-centric teams |
| **Distinctive feature** | TDD + Pair programming | Class ownership | MoSCoW + Timeboxing | Methodology family matching team size |

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1. Define Extreme Programming (XP) and list its **four core values**.  
`[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
Extreme Programming (XP) is a lightweight Agile software development methodology created by **Kent Beck (1999)**, characterized by short development cycles, incremental planning, and continuous testing. The four core values are:

1. **Communication** — open, face-to-face exchange of information.
2. **Simplicity** — implement only what is currently needed.
3. **Feedback** — fast feedback loops via testing and customer demos.
4. **Courage** — the discipline to refactor, speak truth, and adapt.

---

### Question 2. What is the **MoSCoW prioritization** technique in DSDM? Explain with an example.  
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
MoSCoW is a requirement-prioritization technique used in DSDM to classify requirements into four categories within a timebox:

- **M (Must have)** — *e.g.*, "User must be able to log in."  
- **S (Should have)** — *e.g.*, "User should be able to reset password via email."  
- **C (Could have)** — *e.g.*, "User could change profile picture."  
- **W (Won't have, this time)** — *e.g.*, "Social-media login is deferred."  

It ensures that within a fixed timebox, only the most business-critical features are delivered first.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**  
*Explain in detail the **Extreme Programming (XP)** methodology. Discuss its **four core values, twelve engineering practices**, and the **phases of the XP project lifecycle** with a neat diagram. *(14 Marks)*  
`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — Four Core Values and Twelve Engineering Practices (7 marks)**

**1. Four Core Values (2 marks):**  
XP, created by Kent Beck, rests on **Communication, Simplicity, Feedback, and Courage**. Communication eliminates the need for elaborate documentation. Simplicity keeps the design minimal. Feedback comes through tests and customer demos. Courage allows the team to refactor and discard bad code.

**2. Twelve Engineering Practices (5 marks):**  
The twelve practices are grouped into four quadrants:

- **Fine-Scale Feedback:** Pair Programming, Planning Game, Test-Driven Development, Whole Team.
- **Continuous Process:** Continuous Integration, Refactoring, Small Releases.
- **Shared Understanding:** Coding Standards, Collective Code Ownership, Simple Design, System Metaphor.
- **Programmer Welfare:** Sustainable Pace.

> Example: In TDD, a developer first writes a **failing unit test**, then writes the minimum code to pass it, then **refactors** for clarity. This cycle (Red → Green → Refactor) ensures 100% test coverage of new code.

**Part (b) — XP Project Lifecycle Phases (7 marks)**

The XP lifecycle comprises **six phases**:

1. **Exploration (1–2 weeks):** Customer writes user stories; team explores technologies.
2. **Planning (1–2 days):** Customer prioritizes stories; team estimates velocity.
3. **Iterations to First Release (1–4 weeks each):** Stories are implemented in short cycles using pair programming and TDD.
4. **Productionizing:** The first working release is hardened, deployed, and tested in production.
5. **Maintenance:** New user stories are added while the system runs in production.
6. **Death:** Customer has no more stories; the project is closed with documentation.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Exploration] --> A2[Planning]
    A2 --> A3[Iterations to First Release]
    A3 --> A4[Productionizing]
    A4 --> A5[Maintenance]
    A5 --> A6{More Stories?}
    A6 -- Yes --> A3
    A6 -- No --> A7[Death]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**  
> Many students *omit the Death phase* from their XP lifecycle diagram, losing 1 full mark. Always include **all six phases** in the flowchart. Also, do not confuse XP roles with Scrum roles — XP has **Coach, Tracker, Customer, Programmer, Consultant, Manager**, while Scrum has **PO, SM, Dev Team**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**  
*Compare and contrast **Feature Driven Development (FDD)** and **Dynamic Systems Development Method (DSDM)**. Describe the **five processes of FDD** and the **nine principles of DSDM** with examples. *(14 Marks)*  
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — Feature Driven Development (7 marks)**

FDD, created by **Jeff De Luca and Peter Coad (1997)**, is a model-driven Agile method that organises work around small, client-valued **features** expressed in the form *"<action> <object> <result>"*. The five FDD processes are:

1. **Develop an Overall Model:** The team creates a high-level object model of the problem domain.
2. **Build a Features List:** Features are listed and grouped into subject areas.
3. **Plan by Feature:** The Chief Programmer assigns features to Class Owners.
4. **Design by Feature:** A design package (sequence diagrams, signatures, tests) is created per feature.
5. **Build by Feature:** The feature is implemented, unit-tested, inspected, and integrated.

> *Example:* For a library system, a feature is *“Issue a book to a registered member and update the due date.”* This is small enough to be completed in a few days.

**Part (b) — DSDM and its Nine Principles (7 marks)**

DSDM, developed by a UK consortium in 1994 and revised as **DSDM Atern (2007)**, is a **timeboxed Agile framework** for rapid application development. Its nine principles are:

1. **Active user involvement is imperative.**
2. **DSDM teams must be empowered to make decisions.**
3. **Frequent delivery of products is the focus.**
4. **Fitness for business purpose is the essential acceptance criterion.**
5. **Iterative and incremental development converges on an accurate solution.**
6. **All changes during development are reversible.**
7. **Requirements are baselined at a high level.**
8. **Testing is integrated throughout the lifecycle.**
9. **Collaboration between all stakeholders is essential.**

> *Example:* A 4-week DSDM timebox for a billing system may allocate **60 % effort to Must-haves** (e.g., invoice generation), **20 % to Should-haves** (e.g., GST calculation), **20 % to Could-haves** (e.g., PDF export), and **0 % to Won't-haves** (e.g., multi-currency).

| Aspect | FDD | DSDM |
|--------|-----|------|
| Unit of work | Feature | Timebox |
| Strength | Scalability for large systems | Fixed deadlines and RAD |
| Customer role | Domain walk-through | Active, on-site user |
| Lifecycle | 5 linear processes | 5 phase Atern framework |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**  
> Students frequently confuse **DSDM timebox** with **Scrum sprint**. A Scrum sprint has flexible scope; a DSDM timebox has **fixed scope priority** and **fixed duration**, with Must-haves protected absolutely. Also, **DSDM Atern** has 5 phases (Feasibility + Foundations + Exploration + Engineering + Deployment) — many students mistakenly write only 4.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **XP** = Kent Beck, **4 values + 12 practices + 6 lifecycle phases**, famous for **Pair Programming** and **TDD**.
- [x] **FDD** = Jeff De Luca + Peter Coad, **5 processes** (Model → List → Plan → Design → Build), works on **client-valued features**.
- [x] **DSDM** = UK Consortium, **9 principles** (memorize all 9), **MoSCoW** (M-S-C-W), **Timeboxes are fixed** in duration, **Must-haves are protected**.
- [x] **Crystal** = Alistair Cockburn, **family of methodologies** color-coded by team size and criticality.
- [x] **Crystal Clear** = 1–6 people, low criticality; **Crystal Orange** = 20–40 people; **Crystal Maroon** = 80+, mission-critical.
- [x] **Comparison Hot Spots:** XP is *engineering-centric*; FDD is *model-centric*; DSDM is *timebox-centric*; Crystal is *human-centric*.
- [x] **For 14-mark questions:** Always draw a labelled lifecycle diagram (Mermaid-style) and include **founder name + year**.
- [x] **For 3-mark questions:** Quote **2–3 crisp points + an example**; avoid long paragraphs.
- [x] **Don't forget the Death phase of XP** — it's a recurring valuation trap.
- [x] **In DSDM**, scope is variable, time is fixed; **in Scrum**, time is fixed (4 weeks), scope is variable. Both are similar but DSDM has *MoSCoW* and *9 principles* — Scrum does not.
- [x] **Crystal's seven core properties** (Frequent Delivery, Reflective Improvement, Close Communication, Personal Safety, Focus, Easy Access to Expert Users, Technical Environment) — appear in KTU Part A frequently.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare" two methodologies, always present a **2-column table** covering at least 5 dimensions (Founder, Year, Core Unit, Phases, Customer Role, Strength). This single technique can earn 1–2 extra marks over paragraph answers, based on KTU 2024 valuation reports.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Scrum?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)**  
> **Scrum** is an **Agile framework** for developing, delivering, and sustaining complex products. It uses an **iterative and incremental** approach, optimizing predictability and controlling risk through regular feedback loops. Scrum employs fixed-length iterations called **Sprints** (typically 2–4 weeks) to deliver potentially shippable product increments.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Kitchen Brigade 🧑‍🍳

Imagine a high-end restaurant kitchen. The **Head Chef** doesn't give a 200-page recipe and disappear for 6 months. Instead:

| Kitchen Role | Scrum Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Restaurant Owner (vision) | **Product Owner** |
| Head Chef (facilitator) | **Scrum Master** |
| Sous Chefs & Cooks (doers) | **Development Team** |
| Each service (Lunch / Dinner) | **Sprint** |
| The menu of dishes | **Product Backlog** |
| Today's prep list | **Sprint Backlog** |
| Customer feedback after service | **Sprint Review** |
| "What went well / poorly?" meeting | **Retrospective** |

The kitchen produces a **small, edible meal** every service (Increment) rather than waiting 6 months for a wedding banquet that may taste wrong. This is **Scrum** — small, frequent, inspectable deliveries with continuous course correction.

---

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Scrum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every Scrum process rests on **3 Pillars** (must be quoted verbatim in KTU answers):

1. **Transparency** — Significant aspects of the process must be visible to those who perform and receive the work.
2. **Inspection** — Scrum artifacts and progress toward the agreed goal are inspected frequently and diligently.
3. **Adaptation** — If any aspects deviate outside acceptable limits, or the resulting product is unacceptable, the process or the material being processed must be adjusted.

---

## 1.3 The Five Scrum Values (from the Scrum Guide)

- **Commitment** → Team members personally commit to achieving the Sprint goals.
- **Courage** → Team members have courage to do the right thing and work on tough problems.
- **Focus** → Everyone focuses on the work of the Sprint and the goals of the team.
- **Openness** → The team and its stakeholders agree to be open about all work and challenges.
- **Respect** → Team members respect each other to be capable and independent professionals.

---

## 1.4 DevOps in One Line

> [!NOTE]
> **DevOps** is a **culture, practice, and tooling philosophy** that unifies **Development (Dev)** and **Operations (Ops)** to shorten the Software Development Life Cycle and provide **Continuous Delivery** with high software quality. Scrum focuses on *what* to build; DevOps focuses on *how to ship it faster and reliably*.

**Why combine Scrum + DevOps?** Scrum without DevOps = "We built it but can't deploy it." DevOps without Scrum = "We deploy garbage rapidly." Together → **Continuous value delivery**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Scrum Framework — Structural Anatomy

Scrum consists of **3 Roles**, **5 Events**, and **3 Artifacts**. Together they form the **Scrum Triangle** (often depicted with **Sprint** at the center).

### A. The 3 Scrum Roles

| Role | Responsibility | Accountable For |
|---|---|---|
| **Product Owner (PO)** | Maximizes value of the product; manages Product Backlog | Product Backlog ordering & value |
| **Scrum Master (SM)** | Servant-leader; removes impediments; enforces Scrum rules | Process adherence & team well-being |
| **Development Team** | Cross-functional professionals (3–9 members) who deliver the Increment | Delivering a "Done" Increment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Rule:** The Scrum Master is **not** a traditional project manager. A Scrum Master has **no authority** over the team — only over the **process**.

### B. The 5 Scrum Events (Ceremonies)

| # | Event | Time-box | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sprint** | 2–4 weeks | Container for all other events; produces Increment |
| 2 | **Sprint Planning** | ≤ 8 hrs (for 4-wk sprint) | Decide *what* and *how* for the sprint |
| 3 | **Daily Scrum (Stand-up)** | 15 min | Sync team; identify impediments |
| 4 | **Sprint Review** | ≤ 4 hrs (for 4-wk sprint) | Demonstrate Increment to stakeholders |
| 5 | **Sprint Retrospective** | ≤ 3 hrs (for 4-wk sprint) | Inspect & improve the team's process |

### C. The 3 Scrum Artifacts

| Artifact | Contains | Committed By |
|---|---|---|
| **Product Backlog** | Ordered list of everything that *might* be needed | Product Owner |
| **Sprint Backlog** | Sprint Goal + selected items + plan to deliver | Development Team |
| **Increment** | Sum of all Product Backlog items completed in a Sprint + value of previous increments | Development Team |

> Each artifact has a **commitment** (transparency anchor):
> - Product Backlog → **Product Goal**
> - Sprint Backlog → **Sprint Goal**
> - Increment → **Definition of Done (DoD)**

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive — Scrum Terminologies (High-Yield for KTU)

### 2.2.1 Sprint
- A **fixed time-box** (≤ 1 month, ideally 2 weeks) during which a "Done", usable, and potentially releasable product Increment is created.
- **Rules**: Duration is **fixed**; new Sprint starts immediately after previous ends; scope may be clarified/re-negotiated with PO but Sprint Goal cannot be changed.

### 2.2.2 Product Backlog
- An **ordered list of everything** that is known to be needed in the product. It is the **single source of requirements**.
- Items have **attributes**: description, order, estimate, value.
- High-order items are **smaller, clearer, and more certain** than low-order ones (DEEP principle).
- Continuously refined in **Backlog Refinement/Grooming** (≤ 10% of team's capacity).

### 2.2.3 Sprint Backlog
- The set of Product Backlog items selected for the Sprint, plus a **plan for delivering** them and the **Sprint Goal**.
- Owned by the **Development Team**; updated daily as work progresses.
- Highly **visible** in the Sprint Backlog board (To Do → In Progress → Done columns).

### 2.2.4 Sprint Review
- Held at the **end of the Sprint** before the Retrospective.
- The team **demonstrates the Increment** to stakeholders and discusses progress toward the Product Goal.
- **Output**: An updated Product Backlog reflecting new opportunities.
- **NOT a status meeting** — it is a *working meeting*.

### 2.2.5 Sprint Retrospective
- Purpose: Inspect and improve the team's **process, people, tools, definition of Done**, and relationships.
- Three core questions:
  1. What went well?
  2. What didn't go well?
  3. What can we improve?
- **Output**: Most valuable improvement committed for the next Sprint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Retro ≠ Postmortem.** A retrospective is for a *live, ongoing* process. A postmortem is for completed/failed projects. KTU examiners love this distinction.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Agile Metrics)

> [!NOTE]
> Scrum is qualitative, but the following **quantitative metrics** are the most frequently asked in KTU 14-mark calculation questions.

| Metric | Formula | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Velocity (V)** | $V = \dfrac{\sum \text{Story Points Completed}}{\text{Number of Sprints}}$ | Story Points / Sprint | Team's delivery capacity |
| **Sprint Burndown Rate (BDR)** | $BDR = \dfrac{P_0 - P_t}{t}$ where $P_0$ = Initial Points, $P_t$ = Remaining at day $t$ | SP / Day | Track daily progress |
| **Average Velocity (AV)** | $AV = \dfrac{V_1 + V_2 + \dots + V_n}{n}$ | SP / Sprint | Forecast future capacity |
| **Release Forecast** | $\text{Sprints Required} = \dfrac{\text{Remaining Backlog}}{AV}$ | Sprints | Estimate time-to-release |
| **Team Capacity (TC)** | $TC = N \times D \times F$ | Person-Hours | Available effort per sprint |
| **Planned vs Actual Variance** | $\text{Variance} \ \% = \dfrac{\text{Planned} - \text{Actual}}{\text{Planned}} \times 100$ | % | Schedule adherence |
| **Defect Density** | $DD = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{KLOC} \text{ or } \text{Function Points}}$ | / KLOC | Quality metric |
| **Cumulative Flow (CFD)** | $WIP_t = \sum_{i=1}^{k} A_i(t)$ | Tasks | Bottleneck analysis |
| **Cycle Time (CT)** | $CT = T_{\text{done}} - T_{\text{started}}$ | Days | Lead time for one item |
| **Escaped Defects** | $E = \dfrac{\text{Post-release defects}}{\text{Total defects}} \times 100$ | % | Released quality |

Where:
- $N$ = team size, $D$ = working days in sprint, $F$ = **focus factor** (typically **0.6** i.e. **60%** of time on sprint work).
- $A_i(t)$ = tasks in column $i$ on day $t$, $k$ = number of workflow stages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners specifically test **velocity-based forecasting** and **burndown chart interpretation**. Memorize the formulas above; they appear almost every exam.

---

## 2.4 Best Practices of Scrum (Frequently asked as 7-markers)

> [!NOTE]
> The following best practices are the **most-tested list** in KTU 2024 Scheme.

1. **Working software over comprehensive documentation** — The primary measure of progress is a working Increment.
2. **Sprint Goal commitment** — The team commits to a single, coherent Sprint Goal rather than a list of tasks.
3. **Definition of Done (DoD)** — A shared, agreed checklist that defines when an item is complete (e.g., coded, tested, reviewed, documented, deployed).
4. **Time-box everything** — Every event has a strict time-box. Do not let meetings drift.
5. **Cross-functional, self-organizing teams** — No sub-teams or specialist silos; team decides *how* to do the work.
6. **Sustainable pace** — Teams work at a pace they can sustain indefinitely (typically ≤ 40 hrs/week).
7. **Continuous Backlog Refinement** — Spend ~10% of capacity grooming items to keep them ready.
8. **Empirical process control** — Decisions are based on *what is observed*, not on speculation.
9. **Whole-team participation** — PO, SM, and Devs all attend daily scrum, planning, review, and retro.
10. **Limit Work-in-Progress (WIP)** — Pull system; finish work before starting new work.
11. **Visible artifacts** — Sprint board, burndown chart are physically or digitally visible to all.
12. **Inspect & adapt at every event** — No ceremony is a rubber stamp.
13. **Definition of "Done" must include integration & testing** — Avoid "Done means coded."
14. **Single Product Owner with full authority** — Avoid committee-based product ownership.
15. **Retrospective actions are committed & tracked** — No improvement without an owner.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Velocity & Sprint Forecasting

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem (KTU 14-mark style):** A Scrum team has the following completed story points over the last **4 Sprints**: 28, 32, 30, 34. The current Product Backlog contains **240 story points** of work.  
> (a) Calculate the team's **average velocity** and the **standard deviation** to assess predictability.  
> (b) Forecast the **number of Sprints** required to complete the remaining 240 SP.  
> (c) If each Sprint is **2 weeks**, calculate the **expected release date** assuming today is **1st January 2025**.

### Step 1: Calculate Average Velocity

$$AV = \frac{V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + V_4}{4} = \frac{28 + 32 + 30 + 34}{4} = \frac{124}{4} = 31 \text{ SP/Sprint}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Standard Deviation (for KTU "predictability" sub-question)

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(V_i - \mu)^2}$$

We compute each $(V_i - \mu)^2$:

$$(28 - 31)^2 = (-3)^2 = 9$$
$$(32 - 31)^2 = (1)^2 = 1$$
$$(30 - 31)^2 = (-1)^2 = 1$$
$$(34 - 31)^2 = (3)^2 = 9$$

Sum of squares:

$$\sum (V_i - \mu)^2 = 9 + 1 + 1 + 9 = 20$$

Standard deviation:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{20}{4}} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24 \text{ SP}$$

> **Predictability Insight:** $\sigma \approx 2.24$ is low compared to mean 31 → team is **highly predictable** (CV ≈ 7.2%).

### Step 3: Forecast Number of Sprints

$$\text{Sprints Required} = \frac{\text{Remaining Backlog}}{AV} = \frac{240}{31} \approx 7.74 \text{ Sprints}$$

**Practical Rule:** Always round **up** for safety (agile principle: under-promise, over-deliver).

$$\text{Sprints Required} = 8 \text{ Sprints (rounded up)}$$

### Step 4: Calculate Release Date

$$\text{Duration} = 8 \text{ Sprints} \times 2 \text{ weeks/Sprint} = 16 \text{ weeks}$$

Converting 16 weeks from 1st January 2025:

$$16 \text{ weeks} = 4 \text{ months} \approx 112 \text{ days}$$

**Release Date** ≈ **23rd April 2025** (or "end of April 2025").

### Step 5: Confidence-Weighted Forecast (Bonus Marks in KTU)

Using a 90% confidence interval (1.645 $\sigma$ for one-sided):

$$V_{\text{lower}} = AV - 1.645 \times \sigma = 31 - 1.645(2.24) = 27.31 \text{ SP}$$

Worst-case sprints:

$$\text{Sprints}_{\text{worst}} = \frac{240}{27.31} \approx 8.79 \rightarrow 9 \text{ Sprints} = 18 \text{ weeks}$$

> **Answer Summary:** $AV = 31$ SP, $\sigma \approx 2.24$ SP, **8 sprints** (16 weeks) under normal case, **9 sprints** (18 weeks) under 90% confidence → release between **23-Apr-2025 and ~7-May-2025**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Sprint Burndown Chart Calculation

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A 2-week Sprint (10 working days) starts with 60 story points. The team's *ideal* burn rate is 6 SP/day. After 4 days, the team has completed 20 SP but added 5 SP (a defect was found and added to scope).  
> (a) Plot the burndown trend.  
> (b) Calculate the **actual burn rate** and the **projected Sprint completion**.

### Step 1: Establish the Ideal Line (Linear)

$$BDR_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{P_0}{D} = \frac{60}{10} = 6 \text{ SP/day}$$

The ideal line goes from $(0, 60)$ to $(10, 0)$.

### Step 2: Calculate Day-4 Actual State

$$\text{Remaining at Day 4} = P_0 - \text{Completed} + \text{Added} = 60 - 20 + 5 = 45 \text{ SP}$$

### Step 3: Actual Burn Rate at Day 4

$$BDR_{\text{actual}} = \frac{P_0 - P_t}{t} = \frac{60 - 45}{4} = \frac{15}{4} = 3.75 \text{ SP/day}$$

### Step 4: Projected Completion Date

$$\text{Days to Finish} = \frac{P_t}{BDR_{\text{actual}}} = \frac{45}{3.75} = 12 \text{ days}$$

> **Sprint Projection:** 12 days > 10 days → the team will **miss the Sprint Goal by 2 days** unless the burn rate accelerates.

### Step 5: Required Burn Rate to Hit Goal

$$BDR_{\text{required}} = \frac{P_t}{D - t} = \frac{45}{10 - 4} = 7.5 \text{ SP/day}$$

> **Examiner Tip:** Always plot the **ideal line**, **actual line**, and **trend line** on the same graph. KTU typically awards **2 marks** for an accurate burndown chart sketch.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Team Capacity Calculation

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A 7-member team works 8 hours/day. A Sprint is 10 working days. The focus factor is 0.6 (60% of time on Sprint work; rest on meetings, emails, support).  
> Calculate: (a) **Total capacity** in person-hours.  
> (b) If a developer can deliver 4 SP/day at full focus, how many story points can the team commit to?

### Step 1: Total Capacity

$$TC = N \times D \times F \times H_{\text{day}}$$

$$TC = 7 \times 10 \times 0.6 \times 8 = 336 \text{ person-hours}$$

### Step 2: Convert to Story Points

$$\text{SP Capacity} = \frac{TC}{H_{\text{day}} \times \text{SP/day}} = \frac{336}{8 \times 4} = 10.5 \rightarrow 10 \text{ SP/day}$$

For a 10-day sprint:

$$\text{Sprint SP Commitment} = 10.5 \times 10 \approx 105 \text{ SP}$$

> **Insight:** This is how a team *self-commits* in Sprint Planning — they pick items whose SP totals ≤ computed capacity. This is the practical **Definition of Done for capacity**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Frequently Asked in KTU)

> [!TIP]
> This 2-mark difference-table question is asked in almost every KTU Module-4 paper. Memorize verbatim.

| Parameter | **Scrum** | **Traditional (Waterfall)** |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | Iterative & incremental | Sequential, phase-wise |
| Requirements | Evolving, change welcomed | Fixed at start; changes costly |
| Delivery | Frequent increments (every 2–4 weeks) | Single delivery at the end |
| Customer involvement | Continuous | Mostly at start & end |
| Risk | Lower (early detection) | Higher (discovered late) |
| Feedback loop | Every Sprint | After project completion |
| Documentation | Just-enough, working software primary | Heavy, comprehensive |
| Team size | Small (3–9) | Often large, specialized |
| Lead time | Short | Long |
| Suitability | Complex, dynamic, innovative projects | Predictable, well-understood projects |

---

## 3.5 Scrum vs DevOps — Complementary, Not Competing

| Dimension | **Scrum** | **DevOps** |
|---|---|---|
| Primary focus | *What* to build (product value) | *How* to ship it (delivery & operations) |
| Origin | Software development framework | IT operations + development culture |
| Cadence | 2–4 week Sprints | Continuous, hours-to-minutes |
| Scope | Planning → Coding → Testing | Coding → Build → Test → Deploy → Monitor |
| Output | Potentially shippable Increment | Running software in production |
| Roles | PO, SM, Dev Team | Dev, Ops, QA, SRE, Release Manager |
| Tools | Jira, Trello, Azure Boards | Jenkins, GitLab CI, Docker, Kubernetes, Ansible |
| Feedback | Sprint Review | Monitoring, telemetry, user behavior |

> **Synthesis:** Scrum defines the *agile plan*; DevOps automates the *agile delivery*. Together: **Agile + DevOps = Continuous Value Delivery**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
Define **Sprint Backlog**. List any two items it contains.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Sprint Backlog** is the set of **Product Backlog items selected for the Sprint**, plus a **plan for delivering** the product Increment and the **Sprint Goal**.  
> It is owned by the **Development Team** and updated daily during the Sprint.  
> **Two items it contains:**  
> (1) The **Sprint Goal** (single objective for the sprint).  
> (2) The **selected Product Backlog items** (with estimates) and a **plan** (tasks) to convert them into a "Done" Increment.  
> *(1 mark each for definition + 1 mark each for items)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
Distinguish between **Sprint Review** and **Sprint Retrospective** (any three points).

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sprint Review | Sprint Retrospective |
|---|---|
| Held to **inspect the Increment** with stakeholders | Held to **inspect the team's process** internally |
| Output: **Updated Product Backlog** | Output: **Improvement actions** for next Sprint |
| Includes **PO, SM, Devs, Stakeholders** | Includes **only PO, SM, Devs** (Scrum Team) |
| Time-box ≤ 4 hrs (for 4-wk sprint) | Time-box ≤ 3 hrs (for 4-wk sprint) |
| *What* was built | *How* it was built |
*(1 mark per correct difference point)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice**: KTU ESE Module 4 has two alternatives. Answer **ONE** full question.

---

### Q3A. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Modified]** — (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **Scrum framework** in detail. Describe the **3 roles**, **5 events**, and **3 artifacts** with their commitments. (7 marks)

**(b)** A Scrum team completed the following story points in 5 sprints: **20, 25, 22, 28, 30**. The Product Backlog currently has **180 SP** of work remaining. Calculate the **average velocity**, **standard deviation**, and forecast the **number of sprints required** to complete the remaining backlog. If each sprint is 3 weeks long, calculate the **total time-to-completion** in weeks. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Scrum Framework

Scrum is an **Agile framework** that uses an **iterative and incremental** approach to deliver value. It is built on **3 pillars**: *Transparency, Inspection, and Adaptation*, and **5 values**: *Commitment, Courage, Focus, Openness, Respect*.

**3 Roles:**
1. **Product Owner (PO)** — Single person accountable for the **Product Backlog**. Maximizes product value, orders items, expresses stakeholders' needs.
2. **Scrum Master (SM)** — Servant-leader for the Scrum Team. Facilitates events, removes impediments, coaches the team in self-management. **Not a project manager**.
3. **Development Team** — 3–9 cross-functional professionals who deliver a "Done" Increment. Self-organizing; no sub-teams.

**5 Events:**
1. **Sprint** — Time-boxed (≤1 month) container producing Increment.
2. **Sprint Planning** — Defines Sprint Goal & Sprint Backlog.
3. **Daily Scrum** — 15-min daily sync.
4. **Sprint Review** — Inspect Increment with stakeholders.
5. **Sprint Retrospective** — Improve process.

**3 Artifacts & Commitments:**
1. **Product Backlog** → commitment: **Product Goal**.
2. **Sprint Backlog** → commitment: **Sprint Goal**.
3. **Increment** → commitment: **Definition of Done (DoD)**.

*(1 mark per major element × 7 = 7 marks distributed across roles, events, artifacts, pillars.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Velocity & Forecasting Calculation

**Step 1 — Average Velocity (AV):**

$$AV = \frac{20 + 25 + 22 + 28 + 30}{5} = \frac{125}{5} = 25 \text{ SP/Sprint}$$

**Step 2 — Standard Deviation (σ):**

Deviations from mean (25):
$$(20-25)^2 = 25$$
$$(25-25)^2 = 0$$
$$(22-25)^2 = 9$$
$$(28-25)^2 = 9$$
$$(30-25)^2 = 25$$

$$\sum (V_i - \mu)^2 = 25 + 0 + 9 + 9 + 25 = 68$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{68}{5}} = \sqrt{13.6} \approx 3.69 \text{ SP}$$

**Step 3 — Forecast Sprints:**

$$\text{Sprints} = \frac{180}{25} = 7.2 \rightarrow 8 \text{ Sprints (rounded up)}$$

**Step 4 — Total Duration:**

$$\text{Weeks} = 8 \times 3 = 24 \text{ weeks}$$

**Final Answer:** $AV = 25$ SP, $\sigma \approx 3.69$ SP, **8 sprints**, **24 weeks** total. *(1+1+1+2+1+1 = 7 marks)*

---

### Q3B. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Modified]** — (14 Marks) *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain **any seven Best Practices of Scrum** in detail. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the relationship between **Scrum and DevOps**. Why are they considered complementary? List the **DevOps lifecycle phases** and explain **two key DevOps tools** for each of CI and CD. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Best Practices of Scrum

1. **Definition of Done (DoD)** — A shared checklist for completion. Prevents "done-but-broken" increments.
2. **Self-organizing, cross-functional teams** — No micromanagement; team picks *how* to do the work.
3. **Time-boxing every event** — Strictly enforced; protects from meeting fatigue.
4. **Continuous Backlog Refinement** — ≤ 10% capacity groomed; items always "ready".
5. **Sprint Goal focus** — A single objective, not a checklist; gives the team a unifying target.
6. **Sustainable pace** — Typically 40 hrs/week; reduces burnout.
7. **Empirical process control** — Decisions based on observed evidence, not speculation.
8. **Visible artifacts (information radiators)** — Sprint board and burndown chart physically visible.
9. **Retrospective actions are tracked** — Improvement items have owners and deadlines.
10. **Whole-team presence at every event** — PO, SM, Devs attend all ceremonies.

*(1 mark per practice × 7 = 7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Scrum + DevOps Synergy

**Why they are complementary:** Scrum defines **what** to build via iterative planning; DevOps automates **how** to build, test, release, and monitor it. Scrum without DevOps leads to "finished-but-not-deployed" features. DevOps without Scrum leads to "rapidly-shipped-wrong-features". Together → **Continuous Delivery of Business Value**.

**DevOps Lifecycle Phases (8 phases):**
1. **Plan** — Backlog, sprint goals (overlaps with Scrum).
2. **Code** — Feature branching, peer reviews, Git workflows.
3. **Build** — Compile, package via Maven/Gradle.
4. **Test** — Automated unit, integration, performance tests.
5. **Release** — Versioned artifacts to repositories.
6. **Deploy** — Automated rollout to staging/prod.
7. **Operate** — Infrastructure provisioning via IaC.
8. **Monitor** — Telemetry, APM, log analytics.

**Two CI Tools:**
- **Jenkins** — Open-source CI server; declarative pipelines.
- **GitLab CI** — Integrated with Git repositories; YAML-based pipelines.

**Two CD Tools:**
- **Docker** — Containerization; ensures consistent environments.
- **Kubernetes (K8s)** — Container orchestration; auto-scaling, self-healing deployments.
- *(Bonus: Ansible, ArgoCD, Spinnaker)*

*(7 marks distributed: 2 for relationship, 3 for lifecycle, 2 for tools)*

---

## 5.3 CASE STUDY — (For 14-mark Part-B Application Question)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme Case Study]**

### **Case: "FreshBite" — Online Food Delivery Startup**

FreshBite is a startup that wants to build an **AI-based food recommendation mobile app** in **6 months**. The CEO has 5 software engineers, 1 designer, and 1 marketing person. They initially planned a **traditional waterfall** approach but realized the market is highly dynamic and customer feedback changes weekly. They hire you as a **Software Project Management Consultant**.

### Case Study Questions:

**(a)** Advise the CEO on **why Scrum is more suitable than Waterfall** for this scenario. List **5 specific reasons** with reference to the FreshBite context. (5 marks)

**(b)** Design a **Scrum Team structure** for FreshBite. Identify the **Product Owner, Scrum Master, and Development Team** with rationale. (3 marks)

**(c)** Write a sample **Product Backlog** (top 7 items) in priority order with **User Story format** and a sample **Sprint Goal** for Sprint-1. (4 marks)

**(d)** Describe what the team should do in **Sprint Retrospective #3** if they discover: (i) the **Daily Standup is taking 45 minutes** instead of 15, and (ii) **defects are being found late in QA**. Suggest **two concrete improvements**. (2 marks)

---

### Model Solution — Case Study

**(a) Why Scrum over Waterfall (5 marks):**

1. **Dynamic market** — Customer food preferences change weekly; Scrum's iterative feedback accommodates this, while Waterfall's locked requirements would lead to a stale app.
2. **Need for early customer validation** — Scrum's Sprint Review every 2 weeks allows the CEO to demo to real users and pivot quickly.
3. **Innovative, undefined AI/ML features** — Recommendation algorithms need empirical tuning; Scrum's empirical process control allows learning from data continuously.
4. **Small cross-functional team** — Waterfall assumes handoffs between specialized silos; Scrum thrives with a 5-7 person cross-functional team.
5. **Risk reduction** — A bad feature detected in Sprint 2 is cheaper to fix than a bad feature found 6 months later in Waterfall UAT.

**(b) Scrum Team Structure (3 marks):**

- **Product Owner** → The **Marketing Person + CEO** (jointly, but PO role is one person → **CEO** as primary PO, marketing person as proxy). They own the **business value** of features.
- **Scrum Master** → One of the **5 engineers with leadership qualities** (designate 1 as SM part-time, since team is small). Facilitates ceremonies, removes blockers.
- **Development Team** → 4 remaining engineers + 1 designer = **5 cross-functional members** (within 3–9 limit). They self-organize, build, test, and deploy.

**(c) Sample Product Backlog (Top 7) and Sprint-1 Goal (4 marks):**

| # | User Story | Story Points |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | *As a* user, *I want* to sign up via Google/Email, *so that* I can access the app. | 3 |
| 2 | *As a* user, *I want* to see a list of nearby restaurants, *so that* I can pick one. | 5 |
| 3 | *As a* user, *I want* to view a menu with prices and images, *so that* I can order. | 8 |
| 4 | *As a* user, *I want* to add items to a cart, *so that* I can place an order. | 5 |
| 5 | *As a* user, *I want* to pay via UPI / Card, *so that* my order is confirmed. | 8 |
| 6 | *As a* user, *I want* to track my delivery in real-time on a map, *so that* I know ETA. | 13 |
| 7 | *As a* user, *I want* AI-based "Top Picks for You" recommendations, *so that* I discover new food. | 13 |

**Sprint-1 Goal:** *"Enable users to register, browse restaurants, and view menus in a 2-week MVP."*  
**Sprint-1 Commitment:** Stories 1, 2, 3, 4 → Total **21 SP** (assuming team velocity ≈ 20–25).

**(d) Retrospective #3 — Improvements (2 marks):**

- **Impediment 1: Standup taking 45 mins** → Apply the **"Walk-the-Board"** technique: only discuss cards on the **Sprint Backlog** in *To Do, In Progress, Done* columns. Off-topic discussions go to a **"Parking Lot"** for after the standup. Strict **15-min time-box** enforced via timer.
- **Impediment 2: Defects found late in QA** → Adopt **Shift-Left Testing** and **Test-Driven Development (TDD)**. Pair developers with QA in **backlog refinement** so acceptance criteria are unambiguous. Add a **Definition of Done** clause: "≥ 80% unit test coverage before marking a story as Done."

---

## 5.4 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks on Module-4 Scrum Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Sprint Review with Retrospective** — Review = *what was built* (with stakeholders). Retrospective = *how we built it* (team only). Losing **2–3 marks** is common.
> 2. **Forgetting the "commitment" anchor of each artifact** — PO Backlog → Product Goal, Sprint Backlog → Sprint Goal, Increment → DoD. Each MUST be paired.
> 3. **Writing "Scrum Master is a project manager"** — The SM has **no authority over people**, only the **process**. A classical 2-mark killer.
> 4. **Skipping the 3 Pillars / 5 Values** in definition questions — KTU awards 1 mark each for pillars and 1 mark for values.
> 5. **In calculation questions, forgetting to round sprints UP** — Always round up to the next integer sprint (under-promise, over-deliver).
> 6. **Mixing Sprint Burndown with Release Burndown** — Sprint = within a sprint. Release = across multiple sprints. Examiners catch this.
> 7. **Forgetting the time-box** for each event — Time-boxes are part of the Scrum Guide and a favorite 1-mark question.
> 8. **Writing "Scrum and DevOps are the same"** — They are **complementary but distinct**. Scrum = planning/development. DevOps = deployment/operations.
> 9. **PO is a "committee" or "team"** — KTU expects *one* person with full authority. A committee-based PO is anti-pattern.
> 10. **Saying "Sprint Goal can be changed mid-sprint"** — Sprint Goal is **immutable** during a Sprint; only scope may be re-negotiated with PO.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Scrum & DevOps**

### ✅ Core Definitions
- **Scrum**: Agile framework; 2–4 week Sprints; produces Increment.
- **3 Pillars**: Transparency, Inspection, Adaptation.
- **5 Values**: Commitment, Courage, Focus, Openness, Respect.
- **3-3-5 Rule**: 3 Roles, 3 Artifacts, 5 Events.

### ✅ Roles
- **PO** → Owns Product Backlog, maximizes value, single decision-maker.
- **SM** → Servant-leader, removes impediments, enforces rules. **Not** a project manager.
- **Dev Team** → 3–9 cross-functional, self-organizing professionals. **No sub-teams**.

### ✅ Artifacts + Commitments
- **Product Backlog** ↔ **Product Goal**
- **Sprint Backlog** ↔ **Sprint Goal**
- **Increment** ↔ **Definition of Done**

### ✅ Events & Time-Boxes (for 4-week Sprint)
- **Sprint Planning** — ≤ 8 hrs
- **Daily Scrum** — 15 min (daily)
- **Sprint Review** — ≤ 4 hrs
- **Sprint Retrospective** — ≤ 3 hrs
- **Backlog Refinement** — ongoing, ~10% capacity

### ✅ Key Formulas (Calculation Must-Knows)
- $AV = \dfrac{\sum V_i}{n}$
- $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (V_i - \mu)^2}{n}}$
- $\text{Sprints Required} = \dfrac{\text{Remaining Backlog}}{AV}$ (round **up**)
- $BDR = \dfrac{P_0 - P_t}{t}$
- $TC = N \times D \times F \times H$

### ✅ Best Practices (Top 7)
- DoD • Self-organizing teams • Time-boxing • Refinement • Sprint Goal focus • Sustainable pace • Information radiators

### ✅ Scrum vs DevOps
- **Scrum** = *What* to build (planning + development).
- **DevOps** = *How* to ship (CI/CD + operations).
- **DevOps Lifecycle**: Plan → Code → Build → Test → Release → Deploy → Operate → Monitor.
- **CI Tools**: Jenkins, GitLab CI, CircleCI.
- **CD Tools**: Docker, Kubernetes, Ansible, ArgoCD.

### ✅ Case Study Tips
- Always frame Scrum advantages with the **specific case context** (dynamic market, small team, innovative product).
- Sample **User Story format**: *"As a <role>, I want <feature>, so that <benefit>."*
- Sprint Goal is **one sentence** describing the sprint's overarching objective.
- Always pair **impediment → concrete action** in retrospective answers.

### ✅ Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- Don't call SM a "project manager".
- Don't change Sprint Goal mid-Sprint.
- Don't confuse Review vs Retrospective.
- Don't put a single integer sprint count — round up.
- Don't claim Scrum and DevOps are the same — they are **complementary**.
